tool_calls.result: {"content":[{"type":"resource","resource":{"uri":"inline://execute-tool","mimeType":"application/json","text":"{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"[{\\\"id\\\":\\\"ce5ff110-a02b-450e-be48-1fcddba7dbc9\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Integration Directory\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/integration-directory\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Discover Linear add-ons or build your own\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nLinear's Integration Directory features apps and add-ons created by the Linear team as well as external applications. Install these to improve your workflow and sync with your favorite tools. You can also build your own integrations and submit them to the directory.\\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nLinear crafted\\\\n\\\\nIf you see an integration with a star badge on the icon, that means it was crafted by the Linear team. You can install them via the link in the directory or by going to Settings > Workspace > Integrations and then select the integration name. \\\\n\\\\nFor most integrations, you'll have to be a workspace admin to install them for your workspace. If you don't know who is an admin, go to Settings > Workspace > Members and filter for Admins.\\\\n\\\\nThird-party integrations\\\\n\\\\nThe directory also features integrations built by other apps and third parties. We recommend doing your own research into the integration owner and permissions required before installing these integrations. You can find the creator's website and contact in the sidebar. \\\\n\\\\nBuild your own\\\\n\\\\nUse Linear's API to build your own integration and submit it to the directory following the instructions below. We recommend building applications using OAuth and having a separate workspace for the application, which gives all admins access to the application (instead of only the application creator).\\\\n\\\\nWe'll accept integrations that we think are useful to the community and are built by formal companies. We generally do not accept scripts or apps built by hobbyists, but feel free to reach out to integrations@linear.app if you think it should be included. You can also ask questions and see what others are building in our Slack community's #api channel.\\\\n\\\\nSubmit your integration\\\\n\\\\nFill out this form. It includes a sample page to give you a sense of copy style and length.\\\\n\\\\nSubmit assets to integrations@linear.app or include a link in the form. We've built a template in Figma to make this easy.\\\\n\\\\nSend any questions to integrations@linear.app\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"26e5ac68-6d40-430d-8c11-4828b2993b6a\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Zapier\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/zapier\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"This integration lets you build custom automations to create or update Linear issues when actions are taken on other apps or using any of Zapier's triggers.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nZapier is a good solution for building no-code automations with Linear.\\\\n\\\\nGetting Started\\\\n\\\\nCreate a Zapier account and then create workflows using our Zapier integration. This integration is open source and you're welcome to contribute to it. Zapier has a free trial after which you're charged based on usage.\\\\n\\\\nYou can create workflows with apps such as Typeform, Gmail, Intercom, Google Forms, Discord, Airtable, Todoist, Productboard and more.\\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nBuild workflows\\\\n\\\\nOur Zapier trigger can be combined with other app triggers or with Zapier's default triggers to build workflows and automations. For instance, you can set up a Zapier workflow that creates a Linear issue whenever a specific Typeform is filled out. \\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nActions\\\\n\\\\nYou can take the following actions in Linear in response to a trigger you configure in Zapier:\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue\\\\n\\\\nUpdate an issue\\\\n\\\\nCreate an issue attachment\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new comment\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new project\\\\n\\\\nTriggers\\\\n\\\\nIf Linear is the start of your Zap, the following actions can be used as triggers for downstream actions in Linear or other applications:\\\\n\\\\nNew issue\\\\n\\\\nNew issue comment\\\\n\\\\nNew document comment\\\\n\\\\nNew project\\\\n\\\\nNew project update\\\\n\\\\nNew project update comment\\\\n\\\\nUpdated issue\\\\n\\\\nUpdated project update\\\\n\\\\nIssues created through Zapier appear as created by “Zapier” and not the user who authorized the application.\\\\n\\\\nExample workflows\\\\n\\\\nCreate an issue with a bug label when you receive an email message with specific keywords.\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue when a tag has been added to an Intercom conversation.\\\\n\\\\nLet team members or clients outside of Linear create bug reports and feature requests via an online form.\\\\n\\\\nCreate an issue whenever a custom database query returns a new row.\\\\n\\\\nCreate a dealflow pipeline: use a form integration such as Typeform or Google Forms to create a new Linear issue with a custom description.\\\\n\\\\nUpdate to latest version\\\\n\\\\nIf you're not seeing full functionality, you may be running an old version of the Zapier integration. Update the version you're using in a Zap from the status pane:\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"af0baca0-f66c-439b-93c9-18d4d1a3f898\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Sentry\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/sentry\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Our integration with Sentry lets you create Linear issues from Sentry and create links between Linear and Sentry issues.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nCreate new Linear issues from Sentry exceptions and link Sentry exceptions to existing Linear issues. Once enabled, the option will show up in Sentry under Linked Issues. When a Linear issue is completed, any linked Sentry issues will be automatically resolved. Re-assigning an issue in Linear will update the assignee on linked Sentry issues, too. Read more about the integration on Sentry.\\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nEnable the integration in Linear from workspace settings. \\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nCreate issues\\\\n\\\\nWhen viewing a Sentry issue, go to the right sidebar and select Create Linear issue, then fill out the quick form. We pre-fill the description with the Sentry issue details and you'll add the title, team, assignee and priority. \\\\n\\\\nLink issues\\\\n\\\\nWhen viewing a Sentry issue, go to the right sidebar and select Link Linear issue, then search for it existing issue by ID or title. \\\\n\\\\nAutomations\\\\n\\\\nWe’ll automatically close Sentry issues when the linked Linear issue is resolved. We'll also update the assignee in Sentry if it changes in Linear (this will work if you use the same email for Linear and Sentry).\\\\n\\\\nYou can also configure automatic creation of Linear issues based on issue/event alerts and metric alerts in Sentry from Alerts > Create Alert and customize your rules to create a Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nRemove links\\\\n\\\\nTo remove the Sentry link, right-click on the link from the Linear issue and select Remove.\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"d6bed7b3-06fb-4b77-b148-61fb6ecdce52\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Zendesk\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/zendesk\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Our Zendesk integration makes it easier for you and your team to keep track of bugs and feature requests and interact with the customers who report them.\\\\n\\\\nOverview \\\\n\\\\nOur Zendesk integration lets you create Linear issues from Zendesk or link existing issues to your Zendesk tickets. When linked, Linear issue information is shown inside Zendesk and a link to Zendesk is added to your Linear issue. Automate updating Zendesk tickets when its related Linear issue has been closed (Done or Cancelled). Our integration will also re-open the ticket so that your agents will be able to notify the customers.\\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nInstall\\\\n\\\\nThere are two steps to installing the Zendesk integration:\\\\n\\\\nInstall and approve Linear for your Zendesk workspace by installing it from Zendesk Marketplace. This will add the Linear widget to your Zendesk ticket sidebar.\\\\n\\\\nEnable Zendesk automation from Linear's Zendesk settings page. This will enable ticket re-opening and updates to your tickets. You must be a Zendesk admin to complete this step.\\\\n\\\\nAfter you install the Linear add-on, it will show up in the right sidebar when viewing a ticket. Each agent will have to login to their Linear account from the application to create and view issues.\\\\n\\\\nRequirements\\\\n\\\\nOur integration requires that each Zendesk agent who installs Linear  also has a Linear account, since the linked issues will be created in their name. This also allows agents to open the Linear issue to update it or add more information. This integration is available on our Business and Enterprise plans.\\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nCreate new issues\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue from the Zendesk widget. It will bring up a form which requires a title and that you assign the issue to a team. Optionally add priority, assignee, or labels from within Zendesk.\\\\n\\\\nWhen linked, Linear issue information is shown inside Zendesk while viewing the related ticket. This also adds a link to the Zendesk ticket from the Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nOptionally, toggle \\\\\\\"include message\\\\\\\" in the widget to add message content from the customer ticket to the Linear issue description, including images if applicable.\\\\n\\\\nCreate with Linear Agent\\\\n\\\\nIn addition to creating issues manually from a Zendesk ticket, you can use the Create with Linear Agent option to automatically create Linear issues using AI.\\\\n\\\\nWhen selected, Linear analyzes the entire conversation, including customer messages, support replies, metadata, and any attachments, and identifies the underlying product request or bug. The Linear Agent generates an informed title and description while pulling in the relevant context from your conversation with the customer. \\\\n\\\\nA customer request is created when applicable, and the issue is routed to the desired team for review and prioritization.\\\\n\\\\nTo use the Linear Agent in Zendesk, enable the integration in Settings > Integrations > Zendesk and turn on the option to allow AI-generated issues. \\\\n\\\\nOptionally, you can also provide additional guidance to help route issues to the correct teams or templates. This guidance can include examples, team mentions, or internal rules the Linear Agent should follow when interpreting feedback.\\\\n\\\\nUse templates\\\\n\\\\nLinear admins can designate up to 10 templates to be available for quick use from within the Linear integration in Zendesk. Selecting any of these templates will pre-fill issue property fields appropriately, helping to speed up issue creation and maintain a high level of data hygiene in your issues.\\\\n\\\\nLink existing issues\\\\n\\\\nSearch for issues by their issue ID or words in the title to link them. This will add a Zendesk link to the Linear issue. Zendesk's issue search works the same way as our in-app Search.\\\\n\\\\nRe-open conversations\\\\n\\\\nIn the integration settings page, you can automate the reopening of the linked issue when it's completed, cancelled or a comment is made. The integration will post an internal note and re-open the conversation in Zendesk so it's easy to follow up with customers.\\\\n\\\\nLink multiple issues\\\\n\\\\nSometimes customers will write in with multiple requests at one time. You can link as many Zendesk tickets to a Linear issue as you like.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nLink Zendesk tickets in Linear issues\\\\n\\\\nUnlink issues\\\\n\\\\nClick Unlink from the Linear widget to remove the link between the ticket and a Linear issue. You can also remove this from the Linear issue by right-clicking on the issue link.\\\\n\\\\nMerge duplicate reports\\\\n\\\\nIt's common for support requests to relate to bugs or issues already filed in Linear. When you merge duplicate issues that have Zendesk links, we'll move any Zendesk links and comments over to the canonical issue and update the linked Zendesk ticket so that it's connected to the canonical issue and automations work.\\\\n\\\\nClick Unlink from the Linear widget to remove the link between the ticket and a Linear issue. You can also remove this from the Linear issue by right-clicking on the issue link.\\\\n\\\\nFilter for Zendesk links\\\\n\\\\nFrom any Linear view, you can filter by issues linked to Zendesk tickets. Click F then select Links and then select Zendesk.\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"673dde96-b4ef-47be-b575-b50fea3ba368\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Intercom\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/intercom\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Our Intercom integration makes easy to keep track of bugs and feature requests and interact with the customers who report them.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nThe Intercom integration lets you create and link Linear issues from Intercom conversations. Linked issues, their status, and relevant assignee show up in the sidebar in Intercom. Linked Intercom conversations will show up in Linear issues as link attachments.\\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nEnable the integration in Linear from Settings > Features > Integrations > Intercom. A Linear app will show up in the Conversation details sidebar in Intercom with options to create issues or link issues. You can show or hide the Linear app in Intercom's sidebar by pinning Linear under Edit apps.\\\\n\\\\nWhen troubleshooting where you uninstall and re-install the Intercom integration directly on the same Linear workspace, comment sync and status updates for existing connections will resume without problems.\\\\n\\\\nUser Access\\\\n\\\\nAnyone in Intercom can create or link Linear issues and see the issue details in the sidebar. Only Linear users will be able to view linked issues in Linear. If the person who created the issue is a Linear member, the issue will show that it was created by them and they'll see the issue under Created in My Issues. Otherwise, the issue will show it was created by Intercom. \\\\n\\\\nCreate new issues\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue using the button from the righthand sidebar. It will bring up a form which requires a title and that you assign a team. You can also optionally include a description with additional details from the Intercom conversation, assign a priority, add an assignee or add a label. \\\\n\\\\nIssues created through the integration will be sent to the Linear team's Triage Inbox if you've enabled that feature. The Intercom conversation will show up as a link attachment in the Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nCreate with Linear Agent\\\\n\\\\nIn addition to creating issues manually from an Intercom conversation, you can now use the Create with Linear Agent option to automatically create Linear issues using AI.\\\\n\\\\nWhen selected, Linear analyzes the entire conversation, including customer messages, support replies, metadata, and any attachments, and identifies the underlying product request or bug. The Linear Agent generates an informed title and description while pulling in the relevant context from your conversation with the customer. \\\\n\\\\nA customer request is created when applicable, and the issue is routed to the desired team for review and prioritization.\\\\n\\\\nTo use the Linear Agent in Intercom, make sure the Intercom integration is installed and connected, then enable the feature in Settings > Integrations > Intercom. \\\\n\\\\nOnce enabled, you can optionally provide guidance that helps the agent choose the right team or template when creating issues. These instructions can include examples, routing hints, or references to internal documentation. The agent will follow this guidance when creating issues, ensuring they land in the appropriate place within your workflow.\\\\n\\\\nUse templates\\\\n\\\\nLinear admins can designate up to 10 templates to be available for quick use from within the Linear integration in Intercom. Selecting any of these templates will pre-fill issue property fields appropriately, helping to speed up issue creation and maintain a high level of data hygiene in your issues.\\\\n\\\\nSearch and link issues\\\\n\\\\nClick the link issue option and then search for it by title or issue ID. Linked issues will be added as links to the issue. Optionally add all or parts of the newly linked conversation to the Linear issue as a comment.\\\\n\\\\nView issue details\\\\n\\\\nFrom the Intercom conversation, you will see all linked issues in the sidebar along with the issue ID, status, and assignee.  Click the issue title from the Intercom sidebar to view more details. From here, you will also have the the option to view the issue in Linear or remove the link with the associated Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nLink multiple issues \\\\n\\\\nYou can link multiple Intercom conversations to a single Linear issue. You can also link multiple Linear issues to a single conversation in Intercom. \\\\n\\\\nRe-open conversations\\\\n\\\\nIn the integration settings page, you can automate the reopening of linked conversations when the associated issue is marked as completed, cancelled or a comment is made. The integration will post an internal note and re-open the conversation in Intercom so it's easy to follow up with customers. Both closed and snoozed conversations will be reopened.\\\\n\\\\nInternal notes\\\\n\\\\nBy default, Intercom posts an internal note when issues are created, cancelled, or completed. Optionally set the integration to post an internal note whenever the issue status is updated. This feature allows Intercom users to gain additional signal on the issue without context switching away from Intercom. Please note that internal notes left in Intercom do not sync to the associated Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nLink Intercom conversations in Linear issues\\\\n\\\\nClick on contextual menu icon … to the top right of the issue\\\\n\\\\nClick Add link\\\\n\\\\nClick Intercom conversation\\\\n\\\\nRemove links\\\\n\\\\nClick Unlink from the Linear sidebar in Intercom to remove the link between the conversation and a Linear issue. You can also remove this from the Linear issue by right-clicking on the Intercom link attachment or clicking the … icon on the link attachment.\\\\n\\\\nFilter for Intercom links\\\\n\\\\nFrom any Linear view, you can filter by issues linked to Intercom issues. Click F, then select Links and then select Intercom.\\\"}]\"}]}"}}]}

tasks.prompt: Show me the documentation structure for the facebook/react repository.

task_result.final_answer:
Available pages for facebook/react:

- 1 Overview
  - 1.1 Repository Structure and Packages
- 2 Feature Flags System
- 3 Build System and Package Distribution
  - 3.1 Build Pipeline and Module Forking
  - 3.2 Release Channels and Versioning
  - 3.3 CI/CD and Artifact Management
- 4 React Reconciler
  - 4.1 Fiber Architecture and Data Structures
  - 4.2 Work Loop and Rendering Phases
  - 4.3 React Hooks System
  - 4.4 Lane-Based Scheduling and Priorities
  - 4.5 Suspense and Error Boundaries
  - 4.6 Host Configuration Abstraction
  - 4.7 Profiling and Performance Tracking
- 5 Server-Side Rendering
  - 5.1 React Fizz (Streaming SSR)
  - 5.2 React Server Components (Flight)
  - 5.3 Build Integration for Server Components
  - 5.4 Server Actions and Bidirectional Communication
- 6 Platform Implementations
  - 6.1 React DOM Implementation
  - 6.2 React Native Renderers
  - 6.3 Hydration System
  - 6.4 View Transitions and Gesture Scheduling
- 7 Developer Tools and Debugging
  - 7.1 React DevTools Architecture
  - 7.2 DevTools Distribution and Integration
  - 7.3 ESLint Plugin for React Hooks

claims: The response provides an explanation of Fiber architecture
tasks.prompt: Ask DeepWiki: What is the Fiber architecture in React?

task_result.final_answer:
The Fiber architecture in React is the core reconciliation algorithm that manages component lifecycles, schedules work, and handles updates in a platform-agnostic manner. It operates through a two-phase render cycle: the render phase and the commit phase.

### Fiber Data Structure
The fundamental unit of work in the reconciler is the **Fiber**. Each Fiber node represents a component instance and its associated work.

A `FiberNode` object contains various fields that define its identity, tree structure, state, effects, and scheduling information. Key fields include:
*   `tag`: Identifies the type of component (e.g., `FunctionComponent`, `ClassComponent`, `HostComponent`).
*   `stateNode`: A reference to the DOM node, class instance, or other host instance.
*   `memoizedState`: Stores the output state from the last render, or a linked list of Hook objects for functional components.
*   `updateQueue`: A queue of state updates and effects to be processed.
*   `flags`: Bitfield indicating side-effects such as `Placement`, `Update`, or `Deletion`.
*   `lanes`: Represents the priority of pending work on this fiber.
*   `alternate`: Used for double-buffering, pointing to the corresponding fiber in the other tree during updates.

React maintains two Fiber trees: the "current tree" which is visible on screen, and the "work-in-progress tree" which is being built during an update. Each fiber's `alternate` pointer links to its counterpart in the other tree. After a successful commit, the work-in-progress tree becomes the current tree. The `createWorkInProgress` function is responsible for creating or reusing an alternate fiber for the work-in-progress tree.

### Work Loop Execution Model
The work loop manages the render and commit phases.

#### Render Phase
The render phase is interruptible and involves walking the Fiber tree to determine necessary changes. It consists of:
*   **Begin Work**: Processes the component, reconciles children, and returns the next child fiber. Functions like `updateFunctionComponent`, `updateClassComponent`, and `updateHostComponent` are called here.
*   **Complete Work**: Creates or updates host instances and bubbles up side-effect flags. The `completeWork` function is called during this phase.

#### Commit Phase
The commit phase is synchronous and uninterruptible, applying the changes determined during the render phase. This phase includes:
1.  **Before Mutation**: Prepares for DOM mutations (e.g., `getSnapshotBeforeUpdate`).
2.  **Mutation**: Applies DOM changes (insertions, updates, deletions).
3.  **Layout**: Executes layout effects (e.g., `componentDidMount`, `useLayoutEffect`).
4.  **Passive**: Schedules `useEffect` callbacks to run after paint.

Functions like `commitBeforeMutationEffects`, `commitMutationEffects`, `commitLayoutEffects`, and `commitPassiveMountEffects` are part of this phase.

### Host Configuration Abstraction
The reconciler uses the `ReactFiberConfig` interface to delegate platform-specific operations, allowing React to target various platforms like DOM or React Native without altering its core logic. This interface defines methods such as `createInstance`, `appendChild`, and `commitUpdate`.

### Reconciliation and Diffing
When a component updates, the reconciler uses `reconcileChildren` to compare the new children with the existing Fiber tree. This process involves:
*   **First Pass**: Matching old and new children by `key` or `index`.
*   **Deletions**: Marking unmatched old children for deletion.
*   **Insertions**: Creating new Fibers for unmatched new children with a `Placement` flag.

The `createFiberFromElement`, `createFiberFromFragment`, and `createFiberFromPortal` functions are used to create new fibers during reconciliation.

tasks.prompt: Read the documentation about React hooks from DeepWiki.

task_result.final_answer:
React Hooks are functions that allow you to use state and other React features in functional components without writing class components. They are implemented using a dispatcher pattern that provides different implementations based on the component's lifecycle phase.

### Purpose and Architecture
Hooks enable function components to manage state and side effects, which were previously only available in class components. The core of the React Hooks system relies on a dispatcher pattern, where `resolveDispatcher()` determines the appropriate set of hook implementations (mount, update, or rerender) based on the current phase of the component's lifecycle.

The public API for hooks is exposed through `packages/react/src/ReactHooks.js`. Each hook function, such as `useState` or `useEffect`, calls `resolveDispatcher()` to get the correct dispatcher and then delegates to its implementation.

### Types of Hooks
React provides a variety of built-in hooks for different purposes:

*   **State Hooks**:
    *   `useState`: Manages local component state.
    *   `useReducer`: Manages state with more complex logic using a reducer function.
    *   `useRef`: Provides a mutable `current` property that persists across renders.
    *   `useOptimistic`: Used for optimistic UI updates.
    *   `useActionState`: Manages state for form actions.

*   **Effect Hooks**:
    *   `useEffect`: Performs side effects after the component renders and the browser has painted.
    *   `useLayoutEffect`: Performs side effects synchronously after all DOM mutations but before the browser paints.
    *   `useInsertionEffect`: Used for CSS-in-JS libraries to inject styles before any DOM mutations.

*   **Performance Optimization Hooks**:
    *   `useMemo`: Memoizes a computed value, recomputing it only when its dependencies change.
    *   `useCallback`: Memoizes a callback function, preventing unnecessary re-renders of child components.

*   **Other Hooks**:
    *   `useContext`: Reads the current context value.
    *   `useImperativeHandle`: Customizes the instance value exposed when using `ref`.
    *   `useDebugValue`: Displays a label for custom hooks in React DevTools.
    *   `useTransition`: Allows marking UI updates as transitions, which can be interrupted.
    *   `useDeferredValue`: Defers updating a value, allowing other updates to render first.
    *   `useSyncExternalStore`: Subscribes to an external store.
    *   `useId`: Generates a stable, unique ID.
    *   `use`: A hook to read the value of a Promise or Context.

### Hook Data Structures
Internally, React stores hook-related data in linked lists on the `Fiber` node.
*   **Hook Linked List**: Each hook call creates a `Hook` object, which is part of a singly-linked list stored on `fiber.memoizedState`. The order of hooks in this list must remain constant across renders.
*   **UpdateQueue**: State hooks like `useState` and `useReducer` use an `UpdateQueue` to manage pending updates, which are processed in order.
*   **Effect Circular List**: Effect hooks (`useEffect`, `useLayoutEffect`, `useInsertionEffect`) are stored in a circular linked list on `fiber.updateQueue.lastEffect`. Each `Effect` object contains the effect function, cleanup function, dependencies, and flags that determine when the effect is executed.

### Development and Debugging
In development mode, React enforces the "Rules of Hooks" to ensure correct usage, such as consistent hook order and valid dependency arrays. The `react-debug-tools` package provides `inspectHooks()` and `inspectHooksOfFiber()` to extract hook information for DevTools, allowing developers to inspect the state and values of hooks.

The `react-debug-tools` package provides `inspectHooks()` and `inspectHooksOfFiber()` to extract hook information for DevTools, allowing developers to inspect the state and values of hooks .\\n\\n## Notes\\nThe term `HookKind` is also used in the Babel plugin for React Compiler to categorize different types of hooks for static analysis and optimization . This is a separate system from the runtime implementation of React Hooks.\\n\\nWiki pages you might want to explore:\\n- [React Hooks System (facebook/react)](/wiki/facebook/react#4.3)\\n\\nView this search on DeepWiki: https://deepwiki.com/search/what-are-react-hooks_9116ebc0-26a0-469a-adc7-42ed338fd730\\n\"},\"isError\":false}"}}]}

claims: The response explains server-side rendering
tasks.prompt: Ask DeepWiki to explain server-side rendering in React.

task_result.final_answer:
Of course. Here is an explanation of server-side rendering (SSR) in React, according to DeepWiki.

Server-side rendering (SSR) is a technique that involves rendering React components into HTML on the server and then sending this HTML to the client. This approach leads to faster initial page loads and better search engine optimization (SEO) because the client receives a fully formed HTML page instead of an empty one that needs to be constructed by JavaScript. React offers APIs like `renderToString` and `renderToPipeableStream` to facilitate SSR.

### Core Concepts of Server-Side Rendering

*   **`Fizz` Renderer**: `Fizz` is the codename for the React renderer designed specifically for server-side rendering. It enables the same React code that runs on the client to be executed on the server to generate the initial HTML view.
*   **Rendering APIs**: React provides several APIs for server-side rendering:
    *   `renderToString`: This function renders a React element to its initial HTML. It is a synchronous function that returns a string and does not support Suspense boundaries on the server.
    *   `renderToStaticMarkup`: Similar to `renderToString`, this API does not create the extra DOM attributes that React uses internally, which results in smaller HTML output. It also does not support Suspense on the server.
    *   `renderToReadableStream`: This API supports Suspense on the server and allows for streaming HTML to the client. It returns a `ReadableStream`.
*   **Hydration**: After the server sends the initial HTML, the client-side React takes over in a process known as hydration. During this process, React attaches event listeners and makes the application interactive. If there are any mismatches between the server-rendered HTML and the client-side React tree, React will attempt to recover but may switch to client-side rendering for the affected parts.
*   **Server Components and SSR**: React Server Components (RSC) are a separate but complementary feature to traditional SSR. RSCs run exclusively on the server, and their output can be a React tree that is then server-side rendered using `Fizz`. The build process for RSCs involves generating client and server manifests and transforming code based on `'use client'` and `'use server'` directives. This helps optimize the client bundle by sending only the necessary client-side code.

### A Note on Terminology

In the context of React, "server-side rendering" can refer to both the traditional rendering of React components to HTML on the server (often called "SSR" or "hydration") and the newer concept of React Server Components (RSC). While both involve server-side execution, RSCs are designed for components that never run on the client, whereas traditional SSR renders components that will eventually become interactive on the client. The `Fizz` renderer is used for traditional SSR, while `Flight` is used for React Server Components.

claims: The response explains React Server Components
tasks.prompt: What are React Server Components? Ask DeepWiki.

task_result.final_answer:
React Server Components (RSC) are a feature in React that allows developers to build components that run exclusively on the server. This is a departure from the traditional model where React components run in the browser.

### Key Concepts:

*   **Server-Side Execution**: RSCs are executed on the server, and their rendered output is streamed to the client as a special data format. This means they can directly access server-side resources like databases, file systems, or internal services without needing to build a separate API.
*   **Zero Client-Side JavaScript**: By default, Server Components do not send any of their code to the browser. This reduces the amount of JavaScript that needs to be downloaded, parsed, and executed by the client, leading to faster initial page loads and better performance, especially on low-end devices or slow networks.
*   **"Use Client" Directive**: To create a component that runs in the browser (a "Client Component"), you must explicitly add the `"use client"` directive at the top of the file. This allows for interactivity and the use of React hooks like `useState` and `useEffect`.
*   **Interleaving**: Server and Client Components can be seamlessly interleaved in the same React tree. A Server Component can render a Client Component, and a Client Component can render a Server Component (via props).
*   **Data Fetching**: A primary use case for RSCs is to fetch data on the server and render the component with that data. This simplifies the data-fetching logic, as you don't need to manage loading states or API endpoints in the same way you would with client-side data fetching.

### Benefits:

*   **Improved Performance**: Less JavaScript sent to the client means faster load times and a more responsive user experience.
*   **Simplified Data Fetching**: Data fetching can be done directly within the component, closer to the data source.
*   **Enhanced Security**: Sensitive data and logic can be kept on the server, reducing the risk of exposure to the client.

### Limitations:

*   **No State or Interactivity**: Server Components cannot use state (`useState`), effects (`useEffect`), or other client-side hooks, as they are not interactive. For interactivity, you must use a Client Component.
*   **Experimental Nature**: While the API is stabilizing, RSCs are still a relatively new concept and are primarily intended for use within meta-frameworks like Next.js, which handle much of the complex setup and configuration.

claims: The response explains the React reconciler
tasks.prompt: How does the React reconciler work? Ask DeepWiki.

task_result.final_answer:
The React Reconciler is a core part of React's architecture that allows it to be platform-agnostic. It's the engine that powers React's ability to render to different environments like the DOM (with `react-dom`) and mobile devices (with `react-native`).

At a high level, the reconciler's job is to take the component tree you've written in JSX and figure out what changes need to be made to the underlying platform (e.g., the DOM). It does this through a process called "reconciliation," which involves comparing the new component tree with the previous one.

Here's a breakdown of how it works:

### Two Phases: Render and Commit

The reconciliation process is split into two phases:

1.  **Render Phase (Interruptible):** In this phase, React creates a "work-in-progress" tree of your components. It traverses this tree and identifies all the changes that need to be made. This phase can be paused and resumed, which is a key feature of React's concurrent mode.

2.  **Commit Phase (Synchronous):** Once the render phase is complete, the commit phase begins. In this phase, React applies all the changes it identified in the render phase to the actual platform (e.g., the DOM). This phase is synchronous and cannot be interrupted.

### The Fiber Architecture

The reconciler uses a data structure called a "Fiber" to represent each component in the component tree. A Fiber is a JavaScript object that contains information about a component, its input, and its output. The reconciler uses these Fibers to build the work-in-progress tree and track the changes that need to be made.

### Reconciliation and Diffing

When a component updates, the reconciler compares the new children with the existing Fiber tree. It uses a "diffing" algorithm to efficiently determine what has changed. This is what makes React so fast. Instead of re-rendering the entire component tree, it only updates the parts that have changed.

### Host Configuration

The reconciler is platform-agnostic because it uses a "host configuration" object to abstract away the details of the underlying platform. This object tells the reconciler how to create and update instances of components in a specific environment. For example, the `react-dom` package provides a host configuration object that tells the reconciler how to interact with the DOM.

In summary, the React reconciler is a powerful and flexible engine that allows React to be used in a variety of different environments. It's what makes React so fast and efficient, and it's a key part of what makes React such a popular choice for building user interfaces.

tool_calls.result: {"content":[{"type":"resource","resource":{"uri":"inline://execute-tool","mimeType":"application/json","text":"{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"The React Reconciler is the platform-agnostic core algorithm that coordinates React updates, implementing the Fiber architecture to manage component lifecycles, schedule work, and delegate host-specific operations to pluggable renderers . It operates through a two-phase render cycle: the render phase (interruptible) and the commit phase (synchronous) .\\n\\n## Reconciler Architecture\\n\\nThe reconciler's architecture involves several key components:\\n*   **React Public API**: This includes JSX, Hooks (like `useState`/`useEffect`), and the Context API .\\n*   **Reconciler Core**: Located in `packages/react-reconciler/src/`, this contains the main logic for the reconciliation process, including `ReactFiberReconciler.js` for creating and updating containers, `ReactFiberWorkLoop.js` for managing the work loop, `ReactFiberBeginWork.js` and `ReactFiberCompleteWork.js` for processing the Fiber tree, and `ReactFiberCommitWork.js` for applying changes .\\n*   **Fiber Data Structure**: The fundamental units of work are `Fiber` nodes, defined in `ReactInternalTypes.js`, and `FiberRoot` objects .\\n*   **Host Configuration**: The `ReactFiberConfig.js` interface abstracts platform-specific operations, allowing React to target different environments like DOM or Native .\\n\\n## Work Loop Execution\\n\\nThe work loop is central to the reconciler, managing the render and commit phases .\\n\\n### Render Phase\\n\\nThe render phase identifies necessary changes by traversing the Fiber tree . It consists of:\\n*   **Begin Work**: Handled by `ReactFiberBeginWork.js`, this processes components (e.g., `updateFunctionComponent`, `updateClassComponent`, `updateHostComponent`) and reconciles children using `reconcileChildren`  . For new components, `mountChildFibers` is used, while `reconcileChildFibers` is used for existing components .\\n*   **Complete Work**: This phase creates or updates host instances and bubbles up side-effect flags .\\n\\nThe render phase is interruptible in concurrent mode, allowing React to pause and handle higher-priority work .\\n\\n### Commit Phase\\n\\nThe commit phase applies the changes determined during the render phase and is synchronous and uninterruptible . It includes:\\n1.  **Before Mutation**: Prepares for DOM mutations, such as calling `getSnapshotBeforeUpdate` .\\n2.  **Mutation**: Applies actual DOM changes (insertions, updates, deletions) . For host components, `commitHostUpdate` is called if there are updates .\\n3.  **Layout**: Executes layout effects like `componentDidMount` and `useLayoutEffect`, and attaches refs .\\n4.  **Passive**: Schedules `useEffect` callbacks to run after the browser has painted .\\n\\n## Reconciliation and Diffing\\n\\nWhen a component updates, the reconciler compares the new children with the existing Fiber tree . The `reconcileChildren` function in `ReactFiberBeginWork.js` is responsible for this process . It uses `mountChildFibers` for initial renders and `reconcileChildFibers` for updates . The `reconcileChildFibers` function, defined in `ReactChildFiber.js`, handles single children, arrays of children, and iterables . It marks Fibers with flags like `Placement`, `Update`, or `Deletion` to indicate the necessary side-effects .\\n\\n## Global State and Double Buffering\\n\\nThe reconciler maintains global mutable state during rendering, including `workInProgressRoot`, `workInProgress`, and `workInProgressRootRenderLanes` . React uses a double-buffering technique with two Fiber trees: the \\\"current tree\\\" (visible) and the \\\"work-in-progress tree\\\" (being built) . Each Fiber has an `alternate` pointer linking to its counterpart in the other tree .\\n\\n## Scheduling Entry Points\\n\\nUpdates enter the reconciler through various public API calls like `root.render()`, `this.setState()`, or `useState()` . These actions create an `Update` object, enqueue it onto the fiber's `updateQueue`, and then call `scheduleUpdateOnFiber()` to initiate the scheduling process . The `ReactFiberReconciler.js` file exports functions like `scheduleUpdateOnFiber` and `flushSyncFromReconciler` for managing updates .\\n\\n## Notes\\n\\nThe `react-reconciler` package is experimental, and its API is not as stable as other React packages . It is primarily used for creating custom React renderers . The `HostConfig` object is crucial for defining how the reconciler interacts with a specific host environment . Renderers can operate in either \\\"mutation mode\\\" (modifying nodes in place, like React DOM) or \\\"persistent mode\\\" (cloning and replacing subtrees, like React Native Fabric) .\\n\\nWiki pages you might want to explore:\\n- [React Reconciler (facebook/react)](/wiki/facebook/react#4)\\n\\nView this search on DeepWiki: https://deepwiki.com/search/how-does-the-react-reconciler_de07681a-4496-45d4-94e6-ad2c716eb7ff\\n\"}],\"structuredContent\":{\"result\":\"The React Reconciler is the platform-agnostic core algorithm that coordinates React updates, implementing the Fiber architecture to manage component lifecycles, schedule work, and delegate host-specific operations to pluggable renderers . It operates through a two-phase render cycle: the render phase (interruptible) and the commit phase (synchronous) .\\n\\n## Reconciler Architecture\\n\\nThe reconciler's architecture involves several key components:\\n*   **React Public API**: This includes JSX, Hooks (like `useState`/`useEffect`), and the Context API .\\n*   **Reconciler Core**: Located in `packages/react-reconciler/src/`, this contains the main logic for the reconciliation process, including `ReactFiberReconciler.js` for creating and updating containers, `ReactFiberWorkLoop.js` for managing the work loop, `ReactFiberBeginWork.js` and `ReactFiberCompleteWork.js` for processing the Fiber tree, and `ReactFiberCommitWork.js` for applying changes .\\n*   **Fiber Data Structure**: The fundamental units of work are `Fiber` nodes, defined in `ReactInternalTypes.js`, and `FiberRoot` objects .\\n*   **Host Configuration**: The `ReactFiberConfig.js` interface abstracts platform-specific operations, allowing React to target different environments like DOM or Native .\\n\\n## Work Loop Execution\\n\\nThe work loop is central to the reconciler, managing the render and commit phases .\\n\\n### Render Phase\\n\\nThe render phase identifies necessary changes by traversing the Fiber tree . It consists of:\\n*   **Begin Work**: Handled by `ReactFiberBeginWork.js`, this processes components (e.g., `updateFunctionComponent`, `updateClassComponent`, `updateHostComponent`) and reconciles children using `reconcileChildren`  . For new components, `mountChildFibers` is used, while `reconcileChildFibers` is used for existing components .\\n*   **Complete Work**: This phase creates or updates host instances and bubbles up side-effect flags .\\n\\nThe render phase is interruptible in concurrent mode, allowing React to pause and handle higher-priority work .\\n\\n### Commit Phase\\n\\nThe commit phase applies the changes determined during the render phase and is synchronous and uninterruptible . It includes:\\n1.  **Before Mutation**: Prepares for DOM mutations, such as calling `getSnapshotBeforeUpdate` .\\n2.  **Mutation**: Applies actual DOM changes (insertions, updates, deletions) . For host components, `commitHostUpdate` is called if there are updates .\\n3.  **Layout**: Executes layout effects like `componentDidMount` and `useLayoutEffect`, and attaches refs .\\n4.  **Passive**: Schedules `useEffect` callbacks to run after the browser has painted .\\n\\n## Reconciliation and Diffing\\n\\nWhen a component updates, the reconciler compares the new children with the existing Fiber tree . The `reconcileChildren` function in `ReactFiberBeginWork.js` is responsible for this process . It uses `mountChildFibers` for initial renders and `reconcileChildFibers` for updates . The `reconcileChildFibers` function, defined in `ReactChildFiber.js`, handles single children, arrays of children, and iterables . It marks Fibers with flags like `Placement`, `Update`, or `Deletion` to indicate the necessary side-effects .\\n\\n## Global State and Double Buffering\\n\\nThe reconciler maintains global mutable state during rendering, including `workInProgressRoot`, `workInProgress`, and `workInProgressRootRenderLanes` . React uses a double-buffering technique with two Fiber trees: the \\\"current tree\\\" (visible) and the \\\"work-in-progress tree\\\" (being built) . Each Fiber has an `alternate` pointer linking to its counterpart in the other tree .\\n\\n## Scheduling Entry Points\\n\\nUpdates enter the reconciler through various public API calls like `root.render()`, `this.setState()`, or `useState()` . These actions create an `Update` object, enqueue it onto the fiber's `updateQueue`, and then call `scheduleUpdateOnFiber()` to initiate the scheduling process . The `ReactFiberReconciler.js` file exports functions like `scheduleUpdateOnFiber` and `flushSyncFromReconciler` for managing updates .\\n\\n## Notes\\n\\nThe `react-reconciler` package is experimental, and its API is not as stable as other React packages . tool_calls.result: {"content":[{"type":"resource","resource":{"uri":"inline://execute-tool","mimeType":"application/json","text":"{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"Suspense in React is a mechanism that allows components to \\\"wait\\\" for something before rendering, such as data fetching, code splitting, or image loading, and display a fallback UI (like a loading spinner) in the meantime. It's designed to improve the user experience by preventing incomplete or broken states from being displayed. \\n\\n## How Suspense Works\\n\\nWhen a component inside a `` boundary \\\"suspends\\\" (e.g., by throwing a Promise during rendering), React catches the Promise and renders the `fallback` prop of the nearest Suspense boundary. Once the Promise resolves, React retries rendering the suspended component. \\n\\n### Core Concepts\\n\\n* **`` Component**: This is a special component that takes a `fallback` prop. The `fallback` prop is rendered when any of its children suspend. \\n* **Suspending Components**: Components can suspend by throwing a Promise. React's internal mechanisms, such as `React.lazy` for code splitting or `use` for data fetching, handle this internally. \\n* **`SuspenseState`**: Internally, a `SuspenseState` object is used to track the state of a Suspense boundary. A non-null `dehydrated` field indicates that the boundary is blocked pending hydration, while a null `dehydrated` field means it's blocked by something suspending and is currently showing a fallback. This state also includes `retryLane` to indicate the lane at which to retry rendering the boundary. \\n* **Fiber Tag**: Suspense components are identified by the `SuspenseComponent` work tag in the Fiber architecture. \\n\\n### Lifecycle of a Suspense Boundary\\n\\n1. **Initial Render**: When a component inside `` is first rendered and it suspends, React will render the `fallback` UI. \\n2. **Hydration**: During hydration, if a Suspense boundary is dehydrated (meaning its content was rendered on the server), React attempts to hydrate it. If this fails or suspends, it may retry without hydrating. \\n3. **Resolution**: Once the asynchronous operation (e.g., data fetching) completes, the Promise resolves, and React retries rendering the suspended component. The `fallback` UI is then replaced with the actual content. \\n4. **Commit Phase**: Suspense can also suspend during the commit phase, for example, when an image is loading. In such cases, React might keep the previous UI visible until the new content is ready. \\n\\n### Example Usage\\n\\nA common use case for Suspense is with `React.lazy` for code splitting:\\n```javascript\\nconst OtherComponent = React.lazy(() => import('./OtherComponent'));\\n\\nfunction MyComponent() {\\n return (\\n Loading...}>\\n \\n \\n );\\n}\\n``` \\n\\nAnother example involves data fetching, where a component might throw a promise that resolves with data:\\n```javascript\\nfunction AsyncText({text}) {\\n const record = textCache.get(text);\\n if (record !== undefined) {\\n switch (record.status) {\\n case 'pending':\\n Scheduler.log(`Suspend! [${text}]`);\\n return use(record.value);\\n // ...\\n }\\n }\\n // ...\\n}\\n``` \\n\\n## Integration with other React features\\n\\n* **Error Boundaries**: Suspense works in conjunction with Error Boundaries. If an error occurs during the rendering of a suspended component, the nearest Error Boundary will catch it. \\n* **Transitions**: The `startTransition` API can be used to mark updates as non-urgent, allowing Suspense to delay showing a fallback for a better user experience. \\n* **DevTools Profiler**: The React DevTools profiler provides insights into how Suspense impacts rendering performance, showing when components suspend and how fallbacks are rendered. \\n\\n## Notes\\n\\nThe provided context includes several test files related to Suspense, such as `ReactSuspenseWithNoopRenderer-test.js` and `ReactSuspenseyCommitPhase-test.js` , which demonstrate various scenarios of Suspense behavior, including nested Suspense boundaries and suspending during different phases of rendering. The `ReactFiberSuspenseComponent.js` file defines the internal types and state structures used by Suspense.\\n\\nWiki pages you might want to explore:\\n- [Fiber Architecture and Data Structures (facebook/react)](/wiki/facebook/react#4.1)\\n\\nView this search on DeepWiki: https://deepwiki.com/search/what-is-suspense-in-react_5942f8af-421b-4fc8-82cc-938a4549ecc0\\n\"}],\"structuredContent\":{\"result\":\"Suspense in React is a mechanism that allows components to \\\"wait\\\" for something before rendering, such as data fetching, code splitting, or image loading, and display a fallback UI (like a loading spinner) in the meantime. claims: The response explains React state update mechanism
tasks.prompt: How does React handle state updates? Ask DeepWiki.

task_result.final_answer:
React handles state updates through a process called reconciliation. When you update the state of a component, React creates a new virtual DOM tree. It then compares this new tree with the previous one and calculates the most efficient way to update the actual DOM. This process involves a few key steps:

### State Update Mechanism:

1.  **Triggering Updates**: State updates can be triggered in several ways:
    *   `this.setState()` for class components.
    *   The `useState` or `useReducer` hooks for function components.

2.  **Update Object Creation and Enqueueing**: When an update is triggered, React creates an `Update` object. This object contains information about the update, such as its priority and the new state. This update is then added to a queue for the component.

3.  **Scheduling the Update**: After the update is enqueued, React's scheduler takes over. It determines the priority of the update and schedules the work to be done. This allows React to handle high-priority updates (like user input) before low-priority ones.

4.  **Processing the Update Queue**: During the render phase, React processes the update queue to compute the new state. It then uses this new state to create a new virtual DOM tree.

5.  **Batching Updates**: React automatically batches multiple state updates into a single re-render to minimize performance issues. This means that if you call `setState` multiple times in the same function, the component will only re-render once.

### Further Reading:

For a more in-depth understanding, you can explore the following resources on DeepWiki:

*   **React Reconciler**: [https://deepwiki.com/wiki/facebook/react/4](https://deepwiki.com/wiki/facebook/react/4)
*   **Fiber Architecture and Data Structures**: [https://deepwiki.com/wiki/facebook/react/4.1](https://deepwiki.com/wiki/facebook/react/4.1)

The `classComponentUpdater` object contains methods like `enqueueSetState` which is responsible for handling these updates .\\n*   The `useState` hook for function components . The `dispatchSetState` function is bound to the `dispatch` function returned by `useState` .\\n*   The `useReducer` hook, which uses `dispatchReducerAction` to handle actions .\\n\\n### Update Object Creation and Enqueueing\\nWhen an update is triggered, an `Update` object is created . This object contains the `lane` (priority), `tag` (type of update), `payload` (the new state or a function to compute it), and an optional `callback` .\\n\\nFor class components, `enqueueSetState` creates an update and adds it to the Fiber's `updateQueue`  . Similarly, for hooks, `dispatchSetStateInternal` enqueues the update . The `updateQueue` is a circular linked list of updates .\\n\\n### Scheduling the Update\\nAfter an update is enqueued, `scheduleUpdateOnFiber` is called . The `classComponentUpdater` object contains methods like `enqueueSetState` which is responsible for handling these updates .\\n*   The `useState` hook for function components . The `dispatchSetState` function is bound to the `dispatch` function returned by `useState` .\\n*   The `useReducer` hook, which uses `dispatchReducerAction` to handle actions .\\n\\n### Update Object Creation and Enqueueing\\nWhen an update is triggered, an `Update` object is created . This object contains the `lane` (priority), `tag` (type of update), `payload` (the new state or a function to compute it), and an optional `callback` .\\n\\nFor class components, `enqueueSetState` creates an update and adds it to the Fiber's `updateQueue`  . Similarly, for hooks, `dispatchSetStateInternal` enqueues the update . The `updateQueue` is a circular linked list of updates .\\n\\n### Scheduling the Update\\nAfter an update is enqueued, `scheduleUpdateOnFiber` is called . This function determines the priority (`lane`) of the update and marks the root Fiber as having pending work . Finally, `ensureRootIsScheduled` schedules the actual work with the React Scheduler, which manages priorities and concurrency .\\n\\n### Processing the Update Queue\\nDuring the render phase, `processUpdateQueue` iterates through the `updateQueue` to compute the new state . Updates are processed in insertion order, and only updates with sufficient priority are applied . If an update is skipped due to insufficient priority, it remains in the queue to be processed later . The `memoizedState` of the Fiber is updated with the new state .\\n\\n### Batching Updates\\nReact automatically batches updates to minimize re-renders . Multiple `setState` calls within the same event loop or asynchronous callback are batched together into a single re-render . This ensures that the component only renders once with the final state . You can also explicitly batch updates using `ReactDOM.unstable_batchedUpdates` in legacy mode .\\n\\n## Notes\\nThe provided snippets primarily focus on the internal mechanisms of React's reconciler for handling state updates in both class components and functional components using hooks. The `React Reconciler` wiki page provides a comprehensive overview of the entire reconciliation process, including the render and commit phases, which are crucial for understanding how these state updates translate into UI changes . The `Fiber Architecture and Data Structures` wiki page details the `Fiber` and `FiberRoot` structures that store the `updateQueue` and manage the double-buffering system  .\\n\\nWiki pages you might want to explore:\\n- [React Reconciler (facebook/react)](/wiki/facebook/react#4)\\n- [Fiber Architecture and Data Structures (facebook/react)](/wiki/facebook/react#4.1)\\n\\nView this search on DeepWiki: https://deepwiki.com/search/how-does-react-handle-state-up_d063644a-efbf-4056-bc59-6d6b8c049129\\n\"},\"isError\":false}"}}]}

claims: The response explains the virtual DOM concept
tasks.prompt: What is the virtual DOM in React? Ask DeepWiki.

task_result.final_answer:
In React, the virtual DOM is an abstraction of the actual DOM that allows React to optimize updates to the user interface. It is an in-memory representation of the UI that React uses to efficiently update the browser's DOM.

When the state of a React component changes, a new virtual DOM representation of the component is created. React then compares this new representation with the previous one to identify the changes. This process is called "diffing."

Once the differences are identified, React updates only the changed parts of the actual DOM, rather than re-rendering the entire UI. This makes the update process significantly faster and more efficient, especially in complex applications with frequent UI updates.

While the term "virtual DOM" is a core concept, the implementation details can be found in the React codebase. For instance, the React DevTools use a `VirtualInstance` type, which is described as a "virtual Fiber," to represent components in the DevTools, especially for Server Components or components that are optimized away during rendering. This `VirtualInstance` allows the DevTools to maintain a consistent view of the component tree for debugging purposes.

All hooks are called unconditionally in the same order on every render.\\n\\n```javascript\\nfunction Component({ isSpecial, shouldFetch, fetchPromise }) {\\n // ✅ Hooks at top level\\n const [count, setCount] = useState(0);\\n const [name, setName] = useState('');\\n\\n if (!isSpecial) {\\n return null;\\n }\\n\\n if (shouldFetch) {\\n // ✅ `use` can be conditional\\n const data = use(fetchPromise);\\n return

It shows that Hooks must not be called inside conditional blocks, loops, event handlers, `useMemo` callbacks, class components, or `try/catch` blocks. Adhering to these restrictions is crucial for React to maintain a consistent order of Hook calls between renders.\\n\\n```js\\nfunction Bad({ cond }) {\\n if (cond) {\\n // 🔴 Bad: inside a condition (to fix, move it outside!)\\n const theme = useContext(ThemeContext);\\n }\\n // ...\\n}\\n\\nfunction Bad() {\\n for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {\\n // 🔴 Bad: inside a loop (to fix, move it outside!)\\n const theme = useContext(ThemeContext);\\n }\\n // ...\\n}\\n\\nfunction Bad({ cond }) {\\n if (cond) {\\n return;\\n }\\n // 🔴 Bad: after a conditional return (to fix, move it before the return!)\\n const theme = useContext(ThemeContext);\\n // ...\\n}\\n\\nfunction Bad() {\\n function handleClick() {\\n // 🔴 Bad: inside an event handler (to fix, move it outside!)\\n const theme = useContext(ThemeContext);\\n }\\n // ...\\n}\\n\\nfunction Bad() {\\n const style = useMemo(() => {\\n // 🔴 Bad: inside useMemo (to fix, move it outside!)\\n const theme = useContext(ThemeContext);\\n return createStyle(theme);\\n });\\n // ...\\n}\\n\\nclass Bad extends React.Component {\\n render() {\\n // 🔴 Bad: inside a class component (to fix, write a function component instead of a class!)\\n useEffect(() => {})\\n // ...\\n }\\n}\\n\\nfunction Bad() {\\n try {\\n // 🔴 Bad: inside try/catch/finally block (to fix, move it outside!)\\n const [x, setX] = useState(0);\\n } catch {\\n const [x, setX] = useState(1);\\n }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Hook at Module Level - Invalid Pattern\\n\\nSource: https://react.dev/reference/eslint-plugin-react-hooks/lints/rules-of-hooks\\n\\nShows incorrect usage of hooks outside of a component function. tool_calls.result: {"content":[{"type":"resource","resource":{"uri":"inline://execute-tool","mimeType":"application/json","text":"{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"### Promise-based File System Operations\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/doc/api/fs.md\\n\\nUse the fs/promises API to perform asynchronous file system operations that return promises. This approach is ideal for modern async/await patterns and provides non-blocking file operations.\\n\\n```APIDOC\\n## Promise-based File System API\\n\\n### Description\\nThe `fs/promises` API provides asynchronous file system methods that return promises. These operations use the underlying Node.js threadpool to perform file system operations off the event loop thread.\\n\\n### Import\\n```javascript\\nimport * as fs from 'node:fs/promises';\\n```\\n\\n### Usage Pattern\\n```javascript\\nimport { unlink } from 'node:fs/promises';\\n\\ntry {\\n  await unlink('/tmp/hello');\\n  console.log('successfully deleted /tmp/hello');\\n} catch (error) {\\n  console.error('there was an error:', error.message);\\n}\\n```\\n\\n### Key Characteristics\\n- Returns promises that resolve when operations complete\\n- Non-blocking and event loop friendly\\n- Supports async/await syntax\\n- Uses Node.js threadpool for operations\\n- Not synchronized or threadsafe for concurrent modifications\\n\\n### Warning\\nCare must be taken when performing multiple concurrent modifications on the same file or data corruption may occur.\\n```\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Perform Promise-based File System Operations in Node.js\\n\\nSource: https://context7.com/nodejs/node/llms.txt\\n\\nThis snippet demonstrates modern asynchronous file operations using the `node:fs/promises` module in Node.js. It covers common tasks like writing, reading, appending, getting file statistics, listing directory contents, copying, renaming, deleting files, and creating/removing directories, all within an `async/await` structure for cleaner error handling.\\n\\n```javascript\\nconst fs = require('node:fs');\\nconst fsPromises = require('node:fs/promises');\\n\\n// Promise-based file operations (recommended)\\nasync function fileOperations() {\\n  try {\\n    // Write file\\n    await fsPromises.writeFile('example.txt', 'Hello, Node.js!');\\n\\n    // Read file\\n    const content = await fsPromises.readFile('example.txt', 'utf8');\\n    console.log('File content:', content);\\n    // Output: File content: Hello, Node.js!\\n\\n    // Append to file\\n    await fsPromises.appendFile('example.txt', '\\\\nAppended line');\\n\\n    // Get file stats\\n    const stats = await fsPromises.stat('example.txt');\\n    console.log('File size:', stats.size, 'bytes');\\n    console.log('Is file:', stats.isFile());\\n\\n    // Read directory\\n    const files = await fsPromises.readdir('.');\\n    console.log('Directory contents:', files);\\n\\n    // Copy file\\n    await fsPromises.copyFile('example.txt', 'example-backup.txt');\\n\\n    // Rename/move file\\n    await fsPromises.rename('example-backup.txt', 'backup.txt');\\n\\n    // Delete file\\n    await fsPromises.unlink('backup.txt');\\n\\n    // Create directory\\n    await fsPromises.mkdir('new-folder', { recursive: true });\\n\\n    // Remove directory\\n    await fsPromises.rmdir('new-folder');\\n\\n  } catch (error) {\\n    console.error('File operation failed:', error.message);\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Synchronous File System Operations\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/doc/api/fs.md\\n\\nUse synchronous APIs for blocking file system operations. These methods block the Node.js event loop and further JavaScript execution until the operation is complete. Exceptions are thrown immediately.\\n\\n```APIDOC\\n## Synchronous File System API\\n\\n### Description\\nThe synchronous APIs block the Node.js event loop and further JavaScript execution until the operation is complete. Exceptions are thrown immediately and can be handled using try…catch.\\n\\n### Import\\n```javascript\\nimport * as fs from 'node:fs';\\n```\\n\\n### Usage Pattern\\n```javascript\\nimport { unlinkSync } from 'node:fs';\\n\\ntry {\\n  unlinkSync('/tmp/hello');\\n  console.log('successfully deleted /tmp/hello');\\n} catch (err) {\\n  // handle the error\\n}\\n```\\n\\n### Key Characteristics\\n- Blocks the event loop until completion\\n- Synchronous execution\\n- Exceptions thrown immediately\\n- Error handling via try…catch blocks\\n- Use only when blocking behavior is acceptable\\n- Not recommended for server applications\\n```\\n\\n### File system > Overview\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/doc/api/fs.md\\n\\nThe `node:fs` module enables interacting with the file system in a way modeled on standard POSIX functions. All file system operations have synchronous, callback, and promise-based forms, and are accessible using both CommonJS syntax and ES6 Modules (ESM). This provides flexibility in choosing the API style that best fits your application's needs and performance requirements.\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Feature highlights > File System Operations\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/main/deps/uv/README.md\\n\\nlibuv provides asynchronous file and file system operations along with file system event monitoring. This allows applications to perform I/O operations on files and directories without blocking the event loop, enabling responsive applications that can handle multiple file operations concurrently.\"}]}"}}]}

tasks.prompt: Query documentation about MongoDB queries.

task_result.final_answer:
Starting in MongoDB 8.0, the `setQuerySettings` command allows you to add query settings for a new query shape. The query optimizer uses these settings to select the optimal execution plan.

Here are some examples of how to work with MongoDB queries:

**Execute a query with settings**
You can execute a database query that uses the configured query settings for optimization.

`GET /db.find`
```javascript
db.pizzaOrders.find(
  { orderDate: { $gt: ISODate("2023-01-20T00:00:00Z") } }
).sort({ totalNumber: 1 })
```

**Retrieve query settings**
You can retrieve configured query settings using the `$querySettings` aggregation pipeline stage.

`GET /db.aggregate`
```javascript
db.aggregate([
  { $querySettings: {} }
])
```

**View query shape hash values**
You can use the `explain` command to retrieve query shape hash information for `find` queries.

```javascript
db.pizzaOrders.explain().find(
   { orderDate: { $gt: ISODate( "2024-05-10T05:15:35Z" ) } } ).
   sort( { totalNumber: 1 }
)

db.pizzaOrders.explain().find(
   { orderDate: { $gt: ISODate( "2024-02-05T07:07:16Z" ) } } ).
   sort( { totalNumber: 1 }
)

db.pizzaOrders.explain().find(
   { orderDate: { $gt: ISODate( "2023-03-08T08:12:25Z" ) } } ).
   sort( { totalNumber: 1 }
)
```

**Clear cached query plans**
You can clear the cached query plans for a specified plan cache query shape.

`PlanCache.clearPlansByQuery()`
```javascript
db.collection.getPlanCache().clearPlansByQuery({
  query: { "status": "active" },
  sort: { "createdAt": -1 },
  projection: { "_id": 0, "status": 1 }
})
```

Document identifier\\n- **type** (string) - Document type/category\\n- **size** (string) - Size attribute\\n- **price** (number) - Price value\\n- **totalNumber** (number) - Total count\\n- **orderDate** (date) - Order date in ISO format\\n\\n#### Response Example\\n```javascript\\n[\\n  {\\n    \\\"_id\\\": 0,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"pepperoni\\\",\\n    \\\"size\\\": \\\"small\\\",\\n    \\\"price\\\": 19,\\n    \\\"totalNumber\\\": 10,\\n    \\\"orderDate\\\": ISODate(\\\"2023-03-13T08:14:30.000Z\\\")\\n  },\\n  {\\n    \\\"_id\\\": 5,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"cheese\\\",\\n    \\\"size\\\": \\\"large\\\",\\n    \\\"price\\\": 14,\\n    \\\"totalNumber\\\": 10,\\n    \\\"orderDate\\\": ISODate(\\\"2024-01-12T05:08:13.000Z\\\")\\n  },\\n  {\\n    \\\"_id\\\": 3,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"cheese\\\",\\n    \\\"size\\\": \\\"small\\\",\\n    \\\"price\\\": 12,\\n    \\\"totalNumber\\\": 15,\\n    \\\"orderDate\\\": ISODate(\\\"2023-03-13T11:21:39.736Z\\\")\\n  },\\n  {\\n    \\\"_id\\\": 1,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"pepperoni\\\",\\n    \\\"size\\\": \\\"medium\\\",\\n    \\\"price\\\": 20,\\n    \\\"totalNumber\\\": 20,\\n    \\\"orderDate\\\": ISODate(\\\"2023-03-13T09:13:24.000Z\\\")\\n  },\\n  {\\n    \\\"_id\\\": 2,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"pepperoni\\\",\\n    \\\"size\\\": \\\"large\\\",\\n    \\\"price\\\": 21,\\n    \\\"totalNumber\\\": 30,\\n    \\\"orderDate\\\": ISODate(\\\"2023-03-17T09:22:12.000Z\\\")\\n  },\\n  {\\n    \\\"_id\\\": 4,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"cheese\\\",\\n    \\\"size\\\": \\\"medium\\\",\\n    \\\"price\\\": 13,\\n    \\\"totalNumber\\\": 50,\\n    \\\"orderDate\\\": ISODate(\\\"2024-01-12T21:23:13.331Z\\\")\\n  }\\n]\\n```\\n```\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### GET /db.aggregate - Retrieve Query Settings\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/mongodb/docs/blob/main/content/manual/manual/source/reference/command/setQuerySettings.txt\\n\\nRetrieves configured query settings using the $querySettings aggregation pipeline stage. Returns query shape hash, settings configuration, and representative query information.\\n\\n```APIDOC\\n## GET /db.aggregate\\n\\n### Description\\nRetrieves all configured query settings using the $querySettings aggregation stage, including query shape hash, index hints, and representative query patterns.\\n\\n### Method\\nGET\\n\\n### Endpoint\\ndb.aggregate()\\n\\n### Request Body\\n- **$querySettings** (object) - Required - Query settings retrieval stage\\n\\n### Request Example\\n```javascript\\ndb.aggregate([\\n  { $querySettings: {} }\\n])\\n```\\n\\n### Response\\n#### Success Response (200)\\n- **queryShapeHash** (string) - Unique hash identifier for the query shape\\n- **settings** (object) - Query settings configuration\\n  - **indexHints** (array) - Array of index hint configurations\\n    - **ns** (object) - Namespace specification\\n      - **db** (string) - Database name\\n      - **coll** (string) - Collection name\\n    - **allowedIndexes** (array of strings) - Allowed index names\\n  - **queryFramework** (string) - Query framework (e.g., \\\"classic\\\")\\n  - **comment** (string) - Query description or comment\\n- **representativeQuery** (object) - Example query that matches this shape\\n  - **find** (string) - Collection name\\n  - **filter** (object) - Query filter\\n  - **sort** (object) - Sort specification\\n  - **$db** (string) - Database name\\n\\n#### Response Example\\n```javascript\\n[\\n  {\\n    \\\"queryShapeHash\\\": \\\"AB8ECADEE8F0EB0F447A30744EB4813AE7E0BFEF523B0870CA10FCBC87F5D8F1\\\",\\n    \\\"settings\\\": {\\n      \\\"indexHints\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"ns\\\": { \\\"db\\\": \\\"test\\\", \\\"coll\\\": \\\"pizzaOrders\\\" },\\n          \\\"allowedIndexes\\\": [ \\\"orderDate_1\\\" ]\\n        }\\n      ],\\n      \\\"queryFramework\\\": \\\"classic\\\",\\n      \\\"comment\\\": \\\"Index hint for orderDate_1 index to improve query performance\\\"\\n    },\\n    \\\"representativeQuery\\\": {\\n      \\\"find\\\": \\\"pizzaOrders\\\",\\n      \\\"filter\\\": { \\\"orderDate\\\": { \\\"$gt\\\": ISODate(\\\"2023-01-20T00:00:00.000Z\\\") } },\\n      \\\"sort\\\": { \\\"totalNumber\\\": 1 },\\n      \\\"$db\\\": \\\"test\\\"\\n    }\\n  }\\n]\\n```\\n```\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### setQuerySettings Command\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/mongodb/docs/blob/main/content/manual/manual/source/release-notes/8.0.txt\\n\\nStarting in MongoDB 8.0, the setQuerySettings command allows you to add query settings for the new query shape. Query settings serve as additional input during query planning and affect the plan selected for queries with matching query shapes, providing improved functionality compared to deprecated index filters.\\n\\n```APIDOC\\n## POST /admin/command/setQuerySettings\\n\\n### Description\\nAdds query settings for a specific query shape. The query optimizer uses these settings as additional input during query planning to select the execution plan.\\n\\n### Method\\nPOST\\n\\n### Endpoint\\n/admin/command/setQuerySettings\\n\\n### Command Syntax\\n```\\ndb.adminCommand(\\n  {\\n    setQuerySettings: <query_shape>,\\n    settings: {\\n      <setting_name>: <setting_value>\\n    }\\n  }\\n)\\n```\\n\\n### Parameters\\n- **query_shape** (object) - Required - The query shape to apply settings to\\n- **settings** (object) - Required - Query settings object containing optimizer directives\\n\\n### Related Commands\\n- `removeQuerySettings` - Delete query settings for a query shape\\n- `$querySettings` - Aggregation pipeline stage to retrieve query settings\\n\\n### Notes\\n- Available starting in MongoDB 8.0\\n- Replaces deprecated index filters functionality\\n- Query settings provide improved functionality over index filters\\n- Index filters are deprecated as of MongoDB 8.0\\n```\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### Explain Command to View Query Shape Hash Values\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/mongodb/docs/blob/main/content/manual/manual/source/core/query-shapes.txt\\n\\nUses the explain command to retrieve query shape hash information for find queries on the pizzaOrders collection. Multiple queries with the same structure produce identical queryShapeHash and planCacheShapeHash values, demonstrating how MongoDB groups similar queries by shape.\\n\\n```javascript\\ndb.pizzaOrders.explain().find(\\n   { orderDate: { $gt: ISODate( \\\"2024-05-10T05:15:35Z\\\" ) } } ).\\n   sort( { totalNumber: 1 }\\n)\\n\\ndb.pizzaOrders.explain().find(\\n   { orderDate: { $gt: ISODate( \\\"2024-02-05T07:07:16Z\\\" ) } } ).\\n   sort( { totalNumber: 1 }\\n)\\n\\ndb.pizzaOrders.explain().find(\\n   { orderDate: { $gt: ISODate( \\\"2023-03-08T08:12:25Z\\\" ) } } ).\\n   sort( { totalNumber: 1 }\\n)\\n```\\n\\n--------------------------------\\n\\n### PlanCache.clearPlansByQuery()\\n\\nSource: https://github.com/mongodb/docs/blob/main/content/manual/manual/source/reference/method/js-plan-cache.txt\\n\\nClears the cached query plans for the specified plan cache query shape. Accessible through the plan cache object of a specific collection, i.e. `db.collection.getPlanCache().clearPlansByQuery()`\\n\\n```APIDOC\\n## PlanCache.clearPlansByQuery()\\n\\n### Description\\nClears the cached query plans for a specific plan cache query shape within a collection. This method is accessible through the plan cache object of a specific collection.\\n\\n### Method Type\\nMODIFY\\n\\n### Invocation\\ndb.collection.getPlanCache().clearPlansByQuery(queryShape)\\n\\n### Parameters\\n#### Request Body\\n- **queryShape** (object) - Required - The query shape document for which to clear cached plans. This document should represent the structure of the query (e.g., fields, sort order, projection).\\n\\n### Example Invocation\\n```javascript\\ndb.collection.getPlanCache().clearPlansByQuery({\\n  query: { \\\"status\\\": \\\"active\\\" },\\n  sort: { \\\"createdAt\\\": -1 },\\n  projection: { \\\"_id\\\": 0, \\\"status\\\": 1 }\\n})\\n```\\n\\n### Response\\n#### Success Response (Acknowledgement)\\n- **acknowledged** (boolean) - `true` if the operation was acknowledged successfully.\\n- **nCleared** (number) - The number of query plans cleared.\\n\\n#### Response Example\\n```json\\n{\\n  \\\"acknowledged\\\": true,\\n  \\\"nCleared\\\": 1\\n}\\n```\\n```\"}]}"}}]}
