tool_calls.result: {"content":[{"type":"resource","resource":{"uri":"inline://execute-tool","mimeType":"application/json","text":"{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"[{\\\"id\\\":\\\"ce5ff110-a02b-450e-be48-1fcddba7dbc9\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Integration Directory\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/integration-directory\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Discover Linear add-ons or build your own\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nLinear's Integration Directory features apps and add-ons created by the Linear team as well as external applications. Install these to improve your workflow and sync with your favorite tools. You can also build your own integrations and submit them to the directory.\\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nLinear crafted\\\\n\\\\nIf you see an integration with a star badge on the icon, that means it was crafted by the Linear team. You can install them via the link in the directory or by going to Settings > Workspace > Integrations and then select the integration name. \\\\n\\\\nFor most integrations, you'll have to be a workspace admin to install them for your workspace. If you don't know who is an admin, go to Settings > Workspace > Members and filter for Admins.\\\\n\\\\nThird-party integrations\\\\n\\\\nThe directory also features integrations built by other apps and third parties. We recommend doing your own research into the integration owner and permissions required before installing these integrations. You can find the creator's website and contact in the sidebar. \\\\n\\\\nBuild your own\\\\n\\\\nUse Linear's API to build your own integration and submit it to the directory following the instructions below. We recommend building applications using OAuth and having a separate workspace for the application, which gives all admins access to the application (instead of only the application creator).\\\\n\\\\nWe'll accept integrations that we think are useful to the community and are built by formal companies. We generally do not accept scripts or apps built by hobbyists, but feel free to reach out to integrations@linear.app if you think it should be included. You can also ask questions and see what others are building in our Slack community's #api channel.\\\\n\\\\nSubmit your integration\\\\n\\\\nFill out this form. It includes a sample page to give you a sense of copy style and length.\\\\n\\\\nSubmit assets to integrations@linear.app or include a link in the form. We've built a template in Figma to make this easy.\\\\n\\\\nSend any questions to integrations@linear.app\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"26e5ac68-6d40-430d-8c11-4828b2993b6a\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Zapier\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/zapier\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"This integration lets you build custom automations to create or update Linear issues when actions are taken on other apps or using any of Zapier's triggers.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nZapier is a good solution for building no-code automations with Linear.\\\\n\\\\nGetting Started\\\\n\\\\nCreate a Zapier account and then create workflows using our Zapier integration. This integration is open source and you're welcome to contribute to it. Zapier has a free trial after which you're charged based on usage.\\\\n\\\\nYou can create workflows with apps such as Typeform, Gmail, Intercom, Google Forms, Discord, Airtable, Todoist, Productboard and more.\\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nBuild workflows\\\\n\\\\nOur Zapier trigger can be combined with other app triggers or with Zapier's default triggers to build workflows and automations. For instance, you can set up a Zapier workflow that creates a Linear issue whenever a specific Typeform is filled out. \\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nActions\\\\n\\\\nYou can take the following actions in Linear in response to a trigger you configure in Zapier:\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue\\\\n\\\\nUpdate an issue\\\\n\\\\nCreate an issue attachment\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new comment\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new project\\\\n\\\\nTriggers\\\\n\\\\nIf Linear is the start of your Zap, the following actions can be used as triggers for downstream actions in Linear or other applications:\\\\n\\\\nNew issue\\\\n\\\\nNew issue comment\\\\n\\\\nNew document comment\\\\n\\\\nNew project\\\\n\\\\nNew project update\\\\n\\\\nNew project update comment\\\\n\\\\nUpdated issue\\\\n\\\\nUpdated project update\\\\n\\\\nIssues created through Zapier appear as created by “Zapier” and not the user who authorized the application.\\\\n\\\\nExample workflows\\\\n\\\\nCreate an issue with a bug label when you receive an email message with specific keywords.\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue when a tag has been added to an Intercom conversation.\\\\n\\\\nLet team members or clients outside of Linear create bug reports and feature requests via an online form.\\\\n\\\\nCreate an issue whenever a custom database query returns a new row.\\\\n\\\\nCreate a dealflow pipeline: use a form integration such as Typeform or Google Forms to create a new Linear issue with a custom description.\\\\n\\\\nUpdate to latest version\\\\n\\\\nIf you're not seeing full functionality, you may be running an old version of the Zapier integration. Update the version you're using in a Zap from the status pane:\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"af0baca0-f66c-439b-93c9-18d4d1a3f898\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Sentry\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/sentry\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Our integration with Sentry lets you create Linear issues from Sentry and create links between Linear and Sentry issues.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nCreate new Linear issues from Sentry exceptions and link Sentry exceptions to existing Linear issues. Once enabled, the option will show up in Sentry under Linked Issues. When a Linear issue is completed, any linked Sentry issues will be automatically resolved. Re-assigning an issue in Linear will update the assignee on linked Sentry issues, too. Read more about the integration on Sentry.\\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nEnable the integration in Linear from workspace settings. \\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nCreate issues\\\\n\\\\nWhen viewing a Sentry issue, go to the right sidebar and select Create Linear issue, then fill out the quick form. We pre-fill the description with the Sentry issue details and you'll add the title, team, assignee and priority. \\\\n\\\\nLink issues\\\\n\\\\nWhen viewing a Sentry issue, go to the right sidebar and select Link Linear issue, then search for it existing issue by ID or title. \\\\n\\\\nAutomations\\\\n\\\\nWe’ll automatically close Sentry issues when the linked Linear issue is resolved. We'll also update the assignee in Sentry if it changes in Linear (this will work if you use the same email for Linear and Sentry).\\\\n\\\\nYou can also configure automatic creation of Linear issues based on issue/event alerts and metric alerts in Sentry from Alerts > Create Alert and customize your rules to create a Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nRemove links\\\\n\\\\nTo remove the Sentry link, right-click on the link from the Linear issue and select Remove.\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"d6bed7b3-06fb-4b77-b148-61fb6ecdce52\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Zendesk\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/zendesk\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Our Zendesk integration makes it easier for you and your team to keep track of bugs and feature requests and interact with the customers who report them.\\\\n\\\\nOverview \\\\n\\\\nOur Zendesk integration lets you create Linear issues from Zendesk or link existing issues to your Zendesk tickets. When linked, Linear issue information is shown inside Zendesk and a link to Zendesk is added to your Linear issue. Automate updating Zendesk tickets when its related Linear issue has been closed (Done or Cancelled). Our integration will also re-open the ticket so that your agents will be able to notify the customers.\\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nInstall\\\\n\\\\nThere are two steps to installing the Zendesk integration:\\\\n\\\\nInstall and approve Linear for your Zendesk workspace by installing it from Zendesk Marketplace. This will add the Linear widget to your Zendesk ticket sidebar.\\\\n\\\\nEnable Zendesk automation from Linear's Zendesk settings page. This will enable ticket re-opening and updates to your tickets. You must be a Zendesk admin to complete this step.\\\\n\\\\nAfter you install the Linear add-on, it will show up in the right sidebar when viewing a ticket. Each agent will have to login to their Linear account from the application to create and view issues.\\\\n\\\\nRequirements\\\\n\\\\nOur integration requires that each Zendesk agent who installs Linear  also has a Linear account, since the linked issues will be created in their name. This also allows agents to open the Linear issue to update it or add more information. This integration is available on our Business and Enterprise plans.\\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nCreate new issues\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue from the Zendesk widget. It will bring up a form which requires a title and that you assign the issue to a team. Optionally add priority, assignee, or labels from within Zendesk.\\\\n\\\\nWhen linked, Linear issue information is shown inside Zendesk while viewing the related ticket. This also adds a link to the Zendesk ticket from the Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nOptionally, toggle \\\\\\\"include message\\\\\\\" in the widget to add message content from the customer ticket to the Linear issue description, including images if applicable.\\\\n\\\\nCreate with Linear Agent\\\\n\\\\nIn addition to creating issues manually from a Zendesk ticket, you can use the Create with Linear Agent option to automatically create Linear issues using AI.\\\\n\\\\nWhen selected, Linear analyzes the entire conversation, including customer messages, support replies, metadata, and any attachments, and identifies the underlying product request or bug. The Linear Agent generates an informed title and description while pulling in the relevant context from your conversation with the customer. \\\\n\\\\nA customer request is created when applicable, and the issue is routed to the desired team for review and prioritization.\\\\n\\\\nTo use the Linear Agent in Zendesk, enable the integration in Settings > Integrations > Zendesk and turn on the option to allow AI-generated issues. \\\\n\\\\nOptionally, you can also provide additional guidance to help route issues to the correct teams or templates. This guidance can include examples, team mentions, or internal rules the Linear Agent should follow when interpreting feedback.\\\\n\\\\nUse templates\\\\n\\\\nLinear admins can designate up to 10 templates to be available for quick use from within the Linear integration in Zendesk. Selecting any of these templates will pre-fill issue property fields appropriately, helping to speed up issue creation and maintain a high level of data hygiene in your issues.\\\\n\\\\nLink existing issues\\\\n\\\\nSearch for issues by their issue ID or words in the title to link them. This will add a Zendesk link to the Linear issue. Zendesk's issue search works the same way as our in-app Search.\\\\n\\\\nRe-open conversations\\\\n\\\\nIn the integration settings page, you can automate the reopening of the linked issue when it's completed, cancelled or a comment is made. The integration will post an internal note and re-open the conversation in Zendesk so it's easy to follow up with customers.\\\\n\\\\nLink multiple issues\\\\n\\\\nSometimes customers will write in with multiple requests at one time. You can link as many Zendesk tickets to a Linear issue as you like.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nLink Zendesk tickets in Linear issues\\\\n\\\\nUnlink issues\\\\n\\\\nClick Unlink from the Linear widget to remove the link between the ticket and a Linear issue. You can also remove this from the Linear issue by right-clicking on the issue link.\\\\n\\\\nMerge duplicate reports\\\\n\\\\nIt's common for support requests to relate to bugs or issues already filed in Linear. When you merge duplicate issues that have Zendesk links, we'll move any Zendesk links and comments over to the canonical issue and update the linked Zendesk ticket so that it's connected to the canonical issue and automations work.\\\\n\\\\nClick Unlink from the Linear widget to remove the link between the ticket and a Linear issue. You can also remove this from the Linear issue by right-clicking on the issue link.\\\\n\\\\nFilter for Zendesk links\\\\n\\\\nFrom any Linear view, you can filter by issues linked to Zendesk tickets. Click F then select Links and then select Zendesk.\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"673dde96-b4ef-47be-b575-b50fea3ba368\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Intercom\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/intercom\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Our Intercom integration makes easy to keep track of bugs and feature requests and interact with the customers who report them.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nThe Intercom integration lets you create and link Linear issues from Intercom conversations. Linked issues, their status, and relevant assignee show up in the sidebar in Intercom. Linked Intercom conversations will show up in Linear issues as link attachments.\\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nEnable the integration in Linear from Settings > Features > Integrations > Intercom. A Linear app will show up in the Conversation details sidebar in Intercom with options to create issues or link issues. You can show or hide the Linear app in Intercom's sidebar by pinning Linear under Edit apps.\\\\n\\\\nWhen troubleshooting where you uninstall and re-install the Intercom integration directly on the same Linear workspace, comment sync and status updates for existing connections will resume without problems.\\\\n\\\\nUser Access\\\\n\\\\nAnyone in Intercom can create or link Linear issues and see the issue details in the sidebar. Only Linear users will be able to view linked issues in Linear. If the person who created the issue is a Linear member, the issue will show that it was created by them and they'll see the issue under Created in My Issues. Otherwise, the issue will show it was created by Intercom. \\\\n\\\\nCreate new issues\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue using the button from the righthand sidebar. It will bring up a form which requires a title and that you assign a team. You can also optionally include a description with additional details from the Intercom conversation, assign a priority, add an assignee or add a label. \\\\n\\\\nIssues created through the integration will be sent to the Linear team's Triage Inbox if you've enabled that feature. The Intercom conversation will show up as a link attachment in the Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nCreate with Linear Agent\\\\n\\\\nIn addition to creating issues manually from an Intercom conversation, you can now use the Create with Linear Agent option to automatically create Linear issues using AI.\\\\n\\\\nWhen selected, Linear analyzes the entire conversation, including customer messages, support replies, metadata, and any attachments, and identifies the underlying product request or bug. The Linear Agent generates an informed title and description while pulling in the relevant context from your conversation with the customer. \\\\n\\\\nA customer request is created when applicable, and the issue is routed to the desired team for review and prioritization.\\\\n\\\\nTo use the Linear Agent in Intercom, make sure the Intercom integration is installed and connected, then enable the feature in Settings > Integrations > Intercom. \\\\n\\\\nOnce enabled, you can optionally provide guidance that helps the agent choose the right team or template when creating issues. These instructions can include examples, routing hints, or references to internal documentation. The agent will follow this guidance when creating issues, ensuring they land in the appropriate place within your workflow.\\\\n\\\\nUse templates\\\\n\\\\nLinear admins can designate up to 10 templates to be available for quick use from within the Linear integration in Intercom. Selecting any of these templates will pre-fill issue property fields appropriately, helping to speed up issue creation and maintain a high level of data hygiene in your issues.\\\\n\\\\nSearch and link issues\\\\n\\\\nClick the link issue option and then search for it by title or issue ID. Linked issues will be added as links to the issue. Optionally add all or parts of the newly linked conversation to the Linear issue as a comment.\\\\n\\\\nView issue details\\\\n\\\\nFrom the Intercom conversation, you will see all linked issues in the sidebar along with the issue ID, status, and assignee.  Click the issue title from the Intercom sidebar to view more details. From here, you will also have the the option to view the issue in Linear or remove the link with the associated Linear issue.\\\\n\\\\nLink multiple issues \\\\n\\\\nYou can link multiple Intercom conversations to a single Linear issue. claims: The response provides an explanation of Fiber architecture
tasks.prompt: Ask DeepWiki: What is the Fiber architecture in React?

task_result.final_answer:
The Fiber architecture in React is a core reconciliation algorithm that manages component lifecycles, schedules work, and handles updates in a platform-agnostic way. It operates through a two-phase render cycle: the render phase and the commit phase.

### Fiber Data Structure

The fundamental unit of work in the reconciler is the **Fiber**. Each Fiber node represents a component instance and its associated work.

A `Fiber` object contains several key fields:
*   `tag`: Identifies the type of component, such as `FunctionComponent`, `ClassComponent`, or `HostComponent`.
*   `stateNode`: A reference to the DOM node, class instance, or other host instance.
*   `memoizedState`: Stores the output state from the last render; for hooks, this is a linked list of Hook objects.
*   `updateQueue`: A queue of state updates and effects to process.
*   `flags`: Bitfield describing side-effects like `Placement`, `Update`, or `Deletion`.
*   `lanes`: Represents the priority lanes for pending work on this fiber.
*   `alternate`: Used for double-buffering, pointing to the corresponding fiber in the other tree during updates.

The `FiberNode` constructor initializes these properties.

### Work Loop Execution Model

The work loop manages the render and commit phases.

#### Render Phase

The render phase is interruptible and involves walking the Fiber tree to determine necessary changes.
*   **Begin Work**: Processes the component, reconciles children using `reconcileChildren`, and returns the next child fiber to process. Functions like `updateFunctionComponent`, `updateClassComponent`, and `updateHostComponent` are involved here.
*   **Complete Work**: Creates or updates host instances and bubbles up side-effect flags from children.

#### Commit Phase

The commit phase is synchronous and uninterruptible, applying the changes identified in the render phase. This phase includes:
1.  **Before Mutation**: Prepares for DOM mutations.
2.  **Mutation**: Applies DOM changes (insertions, updates, deletions).
3.  **Layout**: Handles `componentDidMount`, `useLayoutEffect`, and ref attachments.
4.  **Passive**: Schedules `useEffect` to run after paint.

### Double Buffering

React uses a double-buffering technique with two Fiber trees:
*   **Current tree**: The tree currently displayed to the user.
*   **Work-in-progress tree**: The tree being constructed during an update.

Each Fiber's `alternate` pointer links it to its counterpart in the other tree. After a successful commit, the work-in-progress tree becomes the current tree.

### Component Types

The reconciler handles various component types, each identified by a `WorkTag`. Examples include:
*   `FunctionComponent`
*   `ClassComponent`
*   `HostComponent` (for DOM elements)
*   `HostText` (for text nodes)
*   `SuspenseComponent`

The `createFiberFromTypeAndProps` function determines the appropriate `WorkTag` based on the element's type and props.

claims: The response explains server-side rendering
tasks.prompt: Ask DeepWiki to explain server-side rendering in React.

task_result.final_answer:
Server-side rendering (SSR) in React is the process of rendering React components on the server and sending the generated HTML to the client. This approach offers several benefits, including faster initial page loads and improved SEO, as search engines can easily crawl the server-rendered HTML.

React provides two main systems for server-side rendering:

**Fizz:** This is React's engine for server-side rendering, which generates HTML from your components. It's designed to be efficient and supports streaming, allowing the server to send parts of the HTML to the client as they are rendered, which can significantly improve performance.

**Flight:** This system is used for serializing React Server Components. Server Components are a newer feature in React that allows some components to run exclusively on the server, reducing the amount of JavaScript sent to the client and improving performance. Flight works in conjunction with Fizz to enable the use of Server Components in a server-rendered application.

These two systems can be used together or independently to build server-rendered React applications. By leveraging SSR, you can create a better user experience with faster load times and ensure your application is easily discoverable by search engines.

The most recently created open issue is **ENG-277: Support multi-organization membership and org switching**.

Here are the details:
*   **Identifier**: ENG-277
*   **Title**: Support multi-organization membership and org switching
*   **Status**: Backlog
*   **Created At**: February 19, 2026
*   **URL**: https://linear.app/cobrowser/issue/ENG-277/support-multi-organization-membership-and-org-switching
*   **Description**: The issue addresses the limitation of the current single-organization-per-user model, which causes user lock-in. The plan is to refactor the architecture to allow users to belong to multiple organizations and switch between them. This involves significant changes to the data model, API, and frontend to create a membership-first system aligned with modern B2B SaaS applications.

tool_calls.result: {"content":[{"type":"resource","resource":{"uri":"inline://execute-code","mimeType":"application/json","text":"{\"result\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"{\\\"id\\\":\\\"6391dab7-bbe5-452e-9f1f-af6382ace619\\\",\\\"identifier\\\":\\\"ENG-277\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Support multi-organization membership and org switching\\\",\\\"description\\\":\\\"## Summary\\\\n\\\\nOur current organization model is effectively single-org-per-user and causes lock-in.\\\\n\\\\nToday:\\\\n\\\\n* New users are auto-assigned a tenant on first authenticated request.\\\\n* A user can only belong to one tenant at a time (`users.tenantId`).\\\\n* Accepting an invite to another org is blocked (`User belongs to a different tenant`).\\\\n* There is no org-switching UX.\\\\n\\\\nThis issue refines the architecture to support **multi-organization membership + active organization switching**, aligned with Better Auth org-plugin patterns and modern B2B SaaS implementations.\\\\n\\\\n## Canonical Naming (must stay consistent)\\\\n\\\\n* **Organization (org)** = primary product/API/DB concept.\\\\n* **Org member** = dashboard principal with org role: **owner** or **admin**.\\\\n* **End user** = separate runtime principal (not a dashboard org-member role).\\\\n* **Tenant** is a deprecated legacy term from current code only. Migration scripts may reference it while renaming, but final API + DB naming should be organization-first.\\\\n\\\\n## Why\\\\n\\\\n* Users need personal + multiple org contexts.\\\\n* Current behavior prevents normal collaboration flows.\\\\n* Industry-standard UX (GitHub/Clerk/Linear style switchers) expects multi-membership.\\\\n\\\\n## Current State (from code)\\\\n\\\\n* Single-tenant coupling:\\\\n  * `users.tenantId`: `apps/api/src/db/schema.ts`\\\\n  * global role array `users.roles`: `apps/api/src/db/schema.ts`\\\\n* Auto-create tenant at auth guard time:\\\\n  * `SessionGuard.ensureUserWithTenant()`: `apps/api/src/common/guards/session.guard.ts`\\\\n* Cross-org invite acceptance is blocked:\\\\n  * `ConflictException(\\\\\\\"User belongs to a different tenant\\\\\\\")`: `apps/api/src/organizations/organizations.service.ts`\\\\n* Frontend assumes one org context:\\\\n  * `OrgProvider` loads one `/organizations/me`: `apps/web/app/_components/OrgProvider.tsx`\\\\n\\\\n## External Research Alignment (2025–2026)\\\\n\\\\n### Better Auth org plugin\\\\n\\\\n* Built-in model is membership-based (`member` with `userId`, `organizationId`, `role`) plus invitation support.\\\\n* Built-in active-org model exists (`activeOrganizationId` in session schema).\\\\n* Built-in list + switch primitives exist (`organization.list`, `setActiveOrganization`).\\\\n* Endpoints can default to active organization when `organizationId` is omitted.\\\\n\\\\n### SaaS reference patterns\\\\n\\\\n* Clerk exposes memberships + invitations + `setActive` organization flow.\\\\n* Auth0 recommends org-scoped claims (`org_id`) and strict org/resource segmentation.\\\\n* Multi-tenant SaaS samples (e.g. Logto sample) use org membership + org-level RBAC as baseline.\\\\n\\\\n## Decision\\\\n\\\\nAdopt a **membership-first architecture** and **normalize naming to organization in both API and DB**.\\\\n\\\\nConcretely:\\\\n\\\\n1. Rename `tenants` -> `organizations`.\\\\n2. Introduce `organization_memberships` for user↔organization membership and org roles.\\\\n3. Add explicit active-org context (session + user fallback).\\\\n4. Shift authz from global user roles to active org-member role.\\\\n5. Add first-class org switch APIs and Firecrawl-style switcher UX.\\\\n\\\\n## Target Architecture\\\\n\\\\n### Data model (target names)\\\\n\\\\n* `organizations` table (renamed from `tenants`).\\\\n* `organization_memberships` table:\\\\n  * `organization_id` FK -> `organizations.id`\\\\n  * `user_id` FK -> `users.id`\\\\n  * `role` (`owner | admin`)\\\\n  * `status` (`active | invited | suspended`)\\\\n  * `invited_by`, `accepted_at`, `created_at`, `updated_at`\\\\n  * unique `(organization_id, user_id)`\\\\n  * index `(user_id)` for \\\\\\\"my orgs\\\\\\\"\\\\n* `users.active_organization_id` (nullable FK -> `organizations.id`) and model field `activeOrganizationId`.\\\\n* `end_user` remains a separate principal type, not an org-member role.\\\\n\\\\n### Naming migration scope (DB cleanup)\\\\n\\\\n* Rename domain columns from `tenant_id` -> `organization_id` across org-scoped tables.\\\\n* Rename join constraints/index names accordingly.\\\\n* Remove long-term dual naming; migration compatibility is temporary only.\\\\n\\\\n### Guard pipeline\\\\n\\\\nCurrent: `SessionGuard -> RolesGuard`\\\\n\\\\nTarget:\\\\n\\\\n1. `SessionGuard` (identity only; no forced org reassignment)\\\\n2. `OrganizationContextGuard` (resolve active org + membership)\\\\n3. `RolesGuard` (evaluate role from active org membership)\\\\n\\\\n### API surface\\\\n\\\\n* `GET /organizations/mine`\\\\n  * list memberships, org metadata, role, invitation counts.\\\\n* `POST /organizations/switch` `{ organizationId }`\\\\n  * verify membership, set active organization context.\\\\n* `POST /organizations/leave` `{ organizationId }`\\\\n  * leave org (with last-owner protection rules).\\\\n* Keep `GET /organizations/me`\\\\n  * derived from active org membership context.\\\\n\\\\n### Invitation behavior\\\\n\\\\n* Remove hard block for users already in other orgs.\\\\n* Accept invite => **insert org membership** for invited organization.\\\\n* Never remove existing memberships during invite acceptance.\\\\n\\\\n### Frontend UX (inspired by Firecrawl screenshots)\\\\n\\\\n* Add org switcher in sidebar/header:\\\\n  * current org\\\\n  * personal/team list\\\\n  * actions: new team, team settings, invite members\\\\n* Switch refreshes org-scoped data and syncs across tabs.\\\\n* Show pending invites in switcher/settings.\\\\n\\\\n## Migration Plan\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 1: Foundation + naming migration\\\\n\\\\n- [ ] Create `organizations` (or rename `tenants`), `organization_memberships`, and `users.active_organization_id`.\\\\n- [ ] Rename `tenant_id` columns to `organization_id` in org-scoped tables.\\\\n- [ ] Backfill memberships from current `users.tenantId` + roles.\\\\n- [ ] Add migration-safe compatibility layer only during rollout window.\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 2: Context + APIs\\\\n\\\\n- [ ] Add `GET /organizations/mine`, `POST /organizations/switch`, `POST /organizations/leave`.\\\\n- [ ] Add `OrganizationContextGuard` and plumb active organization through request context.\\\\n- [ ] Invalidate session cache on org switch.\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 3: Invitation + authz refactor\\\\n\\\\n- [ ] Convert invitation acceptance to membership creation.\\\\n- [ ] Remove cross-org invite conflict checks.\\\\n- [ ] Move role evaluation to org-membership role (active org).\\\\n- [ ] Patch org-scoping gaps (including agent session methods called out in [#249](https://linear.app/cobrowser/issue/ENG-277/support-multi-organization-membership-and-org-switching)).\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 4: Frontend\\\\n\\\\n- [ ] Ship org switcher component and active-org actions.\\\\n- [ ] Update `OrgProvider` to load org list + active org + switch method.\\\\n- [ ] Add multi-tab sync and stale-data invalidation.\\\\n\\\\n### Phase 5: Cleanup\\\\n\\\\n- [ ] Remove reads of legacy `users.tenantId` for dashboard authz.\\\\n- [ ] Remove global dashboard-role dependency on `users.roles`.\\\\n- [ ] Remove migration compatibility aliases/views after rollout.\\\\n\\\\n## Acceptance Criteria\\\\n\\\\n- [ ] A user can belong to multiple orgs simultaneously.\\\\n- [ ] Switching active org does not require re-login.\\\\n- [ ] Accepting an invite to a second org succeeds.\\\\n- [ ] Authz checks are per active org-member role.\\\\n- [ ] No cross-org resource access after switch (IDOR regression tests pass).\\\\n- [ ] Firecrawl-style switch UX is available and usable on desktop/mobile.\\\\n- [ ] API and DB naming are organization-first in steady state.\\\\n\\\\n## Testing\\\\n\\\\n- [ ] Unit tests for org-membership resolution + active-org selection precedence.\\\\n- [ ] Integration tests for switch endpoint and session/cache invalidation.\\\\n- [ ] E2E tests for multi-org invite/accept/switch/leave flows.\\\\n- [ ] Cross-org authorization regression tests on all org-scoped endpoints.\\\\n\\\\n## Notes\\\\n\\\\n* Current repo uses `better-auth@1.3.34`; latest npm is `1.4.18` (verified on 2026-02-20). Evaluate upgrade timing before deep org-plugin integration.\\\\n\\\\n## References\\\\n\\\\n* Better Auth organization plugin docs:\\\\n  * [https://www.better-auth.com/docs/plugins/organization](<https://www.better-auth.com/docs/plugins/organization>)\\\\n* Better Auth org plugin implementation doc source:\\\\n  * [https://github.com/better-auth/better-auth/blob/main/docs/content/docs/plugins/organization.mdx](<https://github.com/better-auth/better-auth/blob/main/docs/content/docs/plugins/organization.mdx>)\\\\n* Clerk organization memberships + active org switching:\\\\n  * [https://clerk.com/docs/react/hooks/use-organization-list](<https://clerk.com/docs/react/hooks/use-organization-list>)\\\\n  * [https://clerk.com/docs/organizations/viewing-memberships](<https://clerk.com/docs/organizations/viewing-memberships>)\\\\n* Auth0 org claims and org-scoped token validation guidance:\\\\n  * [https://auth0.com/docs/manage-users/organizations/using-tokens](<https://auth0.com/docs/manage-users/organizations/using-tokens>)\\\\n* Multi-tenant sample reference:\\\\n  * [https://github.com/logto-io/multi-tenant-saas-sample](<https://github.com/logto-io/multi-tenant-saas-sample>)\\\\n* Additional Better Auth org architecture write-up:\\\\n  * However, I was able to successfully retrieve the list of all users in the workspace. Here is the list of users and their basic information:

tasks.prompt: What are all the possible issue statuses in Linear?

task_result.final_answer:
Here are all the possible issue statuses in Linear for the "Kontext" team:

*   **Todo** (unstarted)
*   **Canceled** (canceled)
*   **Done** (completed)
*   **In Progress** (started)
*   **Backlog** (backlog)

claims: The response includes documentation about workflows
tasks.prompt: Search Linear documentation for information about workflows.

task_result.final_answer:
Based on my search of the Linear documentation, here is a summary of how workflows are handled:

In Linear, workflows are primarily defined by **Issue Statuses**. Each team can configure its own set of statuses, which represent the different stages an issue can go through from creation to completion.

### Key Concepts of Linear Workflows:

*   **Default Workflow:** The default workflow for a team is: `Backlog` > `Todo` > `In Progress` > `Done` > `Canceled`.
*   **Customizable Statuses:** You can add, edit, reorder, and remove statuses to match your team's specific process. This is done in `Settings -> Teams -> Issue statuses & automations`.
*   **Triage:** There is a special "Triage" status category that acts as an inbox for new issues, which is especially useful when using integrations like Slack or support ticketing systems.
*   **Automations:** Linear offers several automations to streamline your workflow:
    *   **Auto-assign:** You can automatically assign issues to yourself when you create them or move them to a "started" status.
    *   **Git-related automations:** When using the GitHub or GitLab integrations, you can configure Linear to automatically change an issue's status and assign it to you when you copy the git branch name.
    *   **Merge Request automation:** For GitLab and GitHub, you can set up rules to automatically update an issue's status when a linked merge/pull request is created, has a review requested, or is merged. You can even set different automations for different target branches (e.g., `staging` vs. `main`).
    *   **Auto-close and auto-archive:** Issues that haven't been updated for a set period can be automatically closed or archived.
*   **Integrations:** You can enhance your workflows by connecting Linear with other tools. The documentation mentions integrations with Figma and GitLab that help streamline design and development workflows.

### For more detailed information, you can refer to the following documentation pages:

*   **Configuring Workflows (Issue Status):** [https://linear.app/docs/configuring-workflows](https://linear.app/docs/configuring-workflows)
*   **Workflow Automations in Preferences:** [https://linear.app/docs/account-preferences](https://linear.app/docs/account-preferences)
*   **GitLab Integration and Workflows:** [https://linear.app/docs/gitlab](https://linear.app/docs/gitlab)

tool_calls.result: {"content":[{"type":"resource","resource":{"uri":"inline://execute-code","mimeType":"application/json","text":"{\"result\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"[{\\\"id\\\":\\\"96e92cf0-7141-4395-8660-393a8c6a9ddd\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Preferences\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/account-preferences\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Adjust your preferences to help you move faster and personalize your workflow.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nGeneral\\\\n\\\\nThe general preferences offer high-level adjustments for how you want things to appear in Linear.\\\\n\\\\nDefault home view\\\\n\\\\nThis setting lets you set the default view that opens whenever you close or log out of Linear and re-open/re-login. It defaults to your team's Active issues view, which is the view that shows all issues that are in an Unstarted or Started workflow status.\\\\n\\\\nTo change your default home view, open the dropdown menu to select from any of these default views:\\\\n\\\\nAll issues\\\\n\\\\nActive issues\\\\n\\\\nCurrent cycle\\\\n\\\\nInbox\\\\n\\\\nMy Issues\\\\n\\\\nFavorited Views\\\\n\\\\nFavorited Projects\\\\n\\\\nDisplay full names\\\\n\\\\nSelect whether you want to see your team members' full names or the username they have personalized in their Profile section.\\\\n\\\\nFirst day of the week\\\\n\\\\nPersonalize how you want your calendars to display your weeks.\\\\n\\\\nConvert text emoticons into emojis\\\\n\\\\nTurn this toggle on to speed up emoji input.\\\\n\\\\nInterface and theme\\\\n\\\\nAdjust your font size and/or opt for a pointer cursor when hovering over interactive elements.\\\\n\\\\nChoose from our presets or create your own custom theme for your workspace. Select several Light or Dark modes as a preference or choose system preference if you'd like the app to switch based on your computer settings.\\\\n\\\\nYou can also build your own custom theme. Find and use over 70 themes (and counting!) on the open source site linear.style, where you can click to copy and use them. \\\\n\\\\nDesktop application\\\\n\\\\nChoose whether to attempt to open Linear URLs in the desktop app.\\\\n\\\\nSelect whether you want notification badges on your desktop app icon, or remove them to avoid the noise.\\\\n\\\\nChoose whether to enable spell check.\\\\n\\\\nAutomations and workflows\\\\n\\\\nAuto-assign to self\\\\n\\\\nOptionally, enable an automation to always assign issues you create to yourself. You can also configure whether you should be auto-assigned to issues you move to a started status.\\\\n\\\\nIf you don't turn these on, you can always toggle the Create more button or use Cmd/Ctrl Shift Enter when creating issues to create more with the same assignee. We don't have a setting to auto-assign issues to specific users. The best way to accomplish that workflow is to create templates with the assignee field set to a specific user.\\\\n\\\\nGit attachment format\\\\n\\\\nDisplay title, or title and repository for your git attachments.\\\\n\\\\nGit-related automations\\\\n\\\\nIf you've enabled Linear's GitHub or GitLab integrations, you'll likely copy the git branch name with Cmd/Ctrl Shift . to link Linear issues to pull requests. You can set two actions to happen whenever you do:\\\\n\\\\nOn git branch copy, move issue to started status which moves the issue to the first workflow status under the started category (often \\\\\\\"In progress\\\\\\\"). \\\\n\\\\nOn git branch copy, auto-assign to yourself\\\\n\\\\nWe highly recommend these settings for any developers using Linear. In combination, they save you a few clicks every time you begin an issue.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"8d26616f-28d7-4210-af91-79da93ea773a\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"GitLab\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/gitlab\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Linear supports linking your Linear issues to GitLab and automates your merge request workflows.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nLink Linear issues to GitLab merge requests. Automate your MR workflow so that issues update when MRs are drafted, opened, merged and when reviews are requested. You can link single or multiple issues to a specific MR.\\\\n\\\\nConfigure the GitLab integration\\\\n\\\\nWe support linking GitLab merge requests for both hosted and self-hosted installations, as long as your installation is publicly available. \\\\n\\\\nNavigate to Linear Settings > Features > Integrations > GitLab\\\\n\\\\nClick Enable to launch the set-up pop-up.\\\\n\\\\nNavigate to GitLab > User Settings > Personal Access Token or GitLab > Project Settings > Access Token to create an API access token.\\\\n\\\\nAn access token is used to query the GitLab API for further information and also used to post issue linkbacks. Because GitLab doesn't support bot accounts, linkbacks are created under the name of the token owner. It's recommended that you create a new user for Linear to act as the bot account.\\\\n\\\\nWhen creating the token, set the api or read_api scope.\\\\n\\\\nIf the read_api scope is selected, Linear will not post linkbacks to the issue on GitLab merge requests. If using a project access token, the token needs Reporter role or higher.\\\\n\\\\n(Optional) Enable and set a custom URL for your self-hosted GitLab installation without any path (e.g. https://gitlab.yourcompany.com). This URL needs to be accessible to public Internet and is only required for self-hosted installations. The earliest supported version of GitLab is 15.6.\\\\n\\\\nIf you need to grant access to Linear's IPs, they are 35.231.147.226, 35.243.134.228, 34.140.253.14, 34.38.87.206, 34.134.222.122, and 35.222.25.142.\\\\n\\\\nClick Connect.\\\\n\\\\nLinear will generate the Webhook URL after it validates the access token. Copy and paste this URL to GitLab in either:\\\\n\\\\nA Group's Webhook Settings (For a company on GitLab's Premium or Ultimate tiers) to integrate all projects under it.\\\\n\\\\nA Project's Webhook Settings, to individually connect a specific project.\\\\n\\\\nEnable the following Triggers:\\\\n\\\\nPush events\\\\n\\\\nComments\\\\n\\\\nMerge request events\\\\n\\\\nPipeline events\\\\n\\\\nUnder SSL verification, ensure Enable SSL verification is checked\\\\n\\\\nClick Save changes.\\\\n\\\\nLink Merge Requests\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new branch\\\\n\\\\nSet the branch format in Linear Settings > Workspace > Integrations GitLab > Branch format. When viewing or selecting a Linear issue, use the Copy git branch name action or shortcut Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + . It will give you a branch name with the issue ID which you can use to create a new branch in GitLab.\\\\n\\\\nAdd the issue ID in the MR title\\\\n\\\\nInclude the Linear issue ID (e.g. ID-123) in the MR title when creating merge requests.\\\\n\\\\nUse a magic word\\\\n\\\\nAdd a magic word + issue ID in the MR description (e.g. Fixes ENG-123). The integration cannot link MRs via comments or commit messages. The available magic words to link issues are: close, closes, closed, closing fix, fixes, fixed, fixing resolve, resolves, resolved, resolving complete, completes, completed, completing.\\\\n\\\\nTo link MRs to issues without them automating your issue status on MR merge, use the one of the contributing magic words: ref, references, part of, related to, contributes to, towards. When using a non-closing magic word like this, the linked MR or commit will still move the issue through other statuses per Workflow settings, but will not automate the issue's status when the MR or commit merges. Instead, it will only transition the issue through to what’s configured prior to the On MR or commit merge event.\\\\n\\\\nLink multiple Linear issues to an MR\\\\n\\\\nTo link multiple Linear issues to a single Merge Request, or to link a Merge Request after creating it, use the magic word linking method by including multiple closing statements in the MR description (e.g. Fixes ENG-123, DES-5 and ENG-256). Linking will happen after you save your changes. The Linear issue will not close until all Merge Requests have been closed/merged.\\\\n\\\\nMerge Request automation\\\\n\\\\nThe merge request automation allows you to select specific statuses for your Linear issues based on MR changes—saving you the hassle of updating Linear issues manually. \\\\n\\\\nCustomize the MR automation in Settings > Team > Workflow. Since this is a team setting, it must be configured for each team in your workspace.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nReady for merge automation\\\\n\\\\nIf you'd like to use the Merge Request automation to capture passing CI checks, please verify in GitLab that:\\\\n\\\\nYou have enabled Pipeline events under the Webhook settings for any repo part of the GitLab integration.\\\\n\\\\nAll pipelines that Linear should consider when determining if an MR is mergeable need to be merge request pipelines. GitLab documentation is available here.\\\\n\\\\nUnder ‘Merge requests’ in project settings, ‘Pipelines must succeed’ is checked.\\\\n\\\\nCustom merge queues\\\\n\\\\nIf you use a custom merge queue that merges changes and then closes the merge request, be sure to add the externally-merged label before closing it. This tells Linear to treat the merge request as successfully merged even though it was closed.\\\\n\\\\nBranch-specific rules\\\\n\\\\nYou can set custom workflow automations based on particular target branches. For example, you can now configure that when a MR is merged to:\\\\n\\\\nstaging, the issue status should change to “In QA”\\\\n\\\\nmain, the issue status should change to “Deployed”\\\\n\\\\nYou can also override a default rule in a particular branch with “no action” if desired, so that issues linked to a change in that branch will not change status. Branch rules can be specified using regex, e.g: ^fea/.* can set automations for all feature branches.\\\\n\\\\nIssue linkbacks\\\\n\\\\nWhen an issue is linked with a merge request, Linear posts a linkback message as a comment with the issue title and description. All the merge requests are also listed in the issue details in Linear. When your MR is being reviewed in GitLab, see the avatars of up to three most recent reviewers and their review states without leaving Linear. This cross-referencing makes it faster to retain context without jumping between apps. Linkbacks are managed by the scope selected.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-assign and update status\\\\n\\\\nSave yourself a few steps by toggling on our automations in Settings > My Account > Preferences > Behavior.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-assigns the issue to you, and/or\\\\n\\\\nMove the issue to the first Started status (which you can customize in Settings > Team > Workflow) when you copy the git branch name.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-linking\\\\n\\\\nGitLab supports rendering URLs pointing to Linear when you mention issue IDs in GitLab. To configure this functionality, please reference GitLab's documentation. \\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"87abf250-960d-4a50-85cf-c67aa69ded11\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Issue status\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/configuring-workflows\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Set statuses that your issues will move through on each team.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nIssues statuses define the type and order of states that issues can move through from start to completion. These workflows are team-specific and come with a default set and order: Backlog > Todo > In Progress > Done > Canceled. \\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nAdd or edit statuses in a team from Settings -> Teams -> Issue statuses & automations. You will see a list of all statuses in that team and their order. Click the three dots next to each status and select Edit to make changes to the name, color, or description. Create statuses by clicking the + button, or remove statuses (as long as at least one status exists in each category). \\\\n\\\\nStatuses can be rearranged within a category but categories cannot be moved around. To change the order of a status, drag it to a new position.\\\\n\\\\nDefault status\\\\n\\\\nThe default status defines the workflow status that will be applied to newly created issues in your team. This can be overridden by the user when creating an issue, but makes it a lot easier to track and organize new issues that come in. By default, your first Backlog status will be the default status. To change that, hover over a different status in the Backlog or Todo categories and then select Make default.\\\\n\\\\nDuplicate issue status\\\\n\\\\nWhen you mark an issue as a duplicate of another, its status will change to Canceled. You can create a custom status in the Canceled category (e.g. Duplicate) and then configure workflow settings so that the custom status is applied instead. \\\\n\\\\nTriage\\\\n\\\\nTriage is an additional status category that acts as an Inbox for your team. Triage is particularly powerful when combined with other integrations like Asks, Slack, or our support ticketing integrations. Learn more here.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-close and auto-archive\\\\n\\\\nWhen enabled, auto-close will close issues that have not been updated in a set period of time.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-archive controls when issues in the team will be archived. When an issue archives, its creator will be notified—this is an opportunity to unarchive if the issue is still relevant. Archived issues are still searchable and restorable in the future.\\\\n\\\\nArchiving is only automatic and is not available as a manual action. More information about archiving is available here.\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"ce5ff110-a02b-450e-be48-1fcddba7dbc9\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Integration Directory\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/integration-directory\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Discover Linear add-ons or build your own\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nLinear's Integration Directory features apps and add-ons created by the Linear team as well as external applications. Install these to improve your workflow and sync with your favorite tools. You can also build your own integrations and submit them to the directory.\\\\n\\\\nBasics\\\\n\\\\nLinear crafted\\\\n\\\\nIf you see an integration with a star badge on the icon, that means it was crafted by the Linear team. You can install them via the link in the directory or by going to Settings > Workspace > Integrations and then select the integration name. \\\\n\\\\nFor most integrations, you'll have to be a workspace admin to install them for your workspace. If you don't know who is an admin, go to Settings > Workspace > Members and filter for Admins.\\\\n\\\\nThird-party integrations\\\\n\\\\nThe directory also features integrations built by other apps and third parties. We recommend doing your own research into the integration owner and permissions required before installing these integrations. You can find the creator's website and contact in the sidebar. \\\\n\\\\nBuild your own\\\\n\\\\nUse Linear's API to build your own integration and submit it to the directory following the instructions below. We recommend building applications using OAuth and having a separate workspace for the application, which gives all admins access to the application (instead of only the application creator).\\\\n\\\\nWe'll accept integrations that we think are useful to the community and are built by formal companies. We generally do not accept scripts or apps built by hobbyists, but feel free to reach out to integrations@linear.app if you think it should be included. You can also ask questions and see what others are building in our Slack community's #api channel.\\\\n\\\\nSubmit your integration\\\\n\\\\nFill out this form. It includes a sample page to give you a sense of copy style and length.\\\\n\\\\nSubmit assets to integrations@linear.app or include a link in the form. We've built a template in Figma to make this easy.\\\\n\\\\nSend any questions to integrations@linear.app\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"c79e8be3-1aed-4abb-9a69-28f592fcbe89\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Figma\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/figma\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"We use Figma at Linear and our integrated tools make it easy for you to collaborate on design.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nThere are two ways to connect Figma to Linear to streamline your design and development workflows. The Linear plugin for Figma allows you to create and link issues directly from Figma. A separate integration enables you to embed designs into Linear descriptions, comments and documents. \\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nEmbed Figma previews in Linear\\\\n\\\\nConnect to Figma under Settings > Features > Integrations > Figma. We recommend that you do this in a browser and not the desktop client. Once connected, the integration will work across your full workspace.\\\\n\\\\nLinear plugin for Figma\\\\n\\\\nTo create or link issues from Figma, select a frame, section or link and run the Linear plugin from Resources > Plugins > Linear or run directly from the Figma community here.\\\\n\\\\nEmbed Figma Previews\\\\n\\\\nPreview designs\\\\n\\\\nSimply copy a link to specific frames or files and paste it into a Linear issue description or comment. We automatically convert the link into a design preview. Even if the file changes, the snapshot in Linear remains as it appeared when the comment or description was created. \\\\n\\\\nOpen files\\\\n\\\\nClick to preview the embedded file without having to leave Linear. We currently support interactive in-app preview of publicly shared Figma files only. We're considering building support for private files in the future. We built this as a custom integration using Figma's API with their OAuth2 authentication instead of using their standard embed to create a faster experience.\\\\n\\\\nRefresh files\\\\n\\\\nBy default, we do not refresh the Figma preview so that you maintain the context of related comments and issue descriptions. To update the preview, go into Edit mode in the issue or comment, then hover over the Figma file. You'll see a Refresh button appear, which you can click to pull the latest version. This action cannot be undone.\\\\n\\\\nLinear Plugin for Figma\\\\n\\\\nRun the plugin to see all Linear issues linked to your designs. From the plugin, select issues or the linked elements below the issue description to be taken to the inked frame, section, or page.\\\\n\\\\nCreate issues\\\\n\\\\nWhen using the Linear plugin from within Figma, select the + button to create issues that link pages, frames, and sections of your Figma design. You'll be able to create issues in any teams that you've joined. \\\\n\\\\nLink issues\\\\n\\\\nLink designs to existing issues by searching for the ID, title, or description. From the Linear side, you can link designs to issues by attaching Figma links for the page, section, or frame using Ctrl L. You can link multiple issues to the same design and multiple frames, sections, or pages to a single Linear issue. \\\\n\\\\nUpdate issues\\\\n\\\\nUpdate properties such as team, status, assignee, and project directly from Figma. You'll be able to update these at any point during your design process. Any changes made in Linear are immediately synced to the Figma plugin.\\\\n\\\\nFilter and sort\\\\n\\\\nFilter your issues that appear in the plugin window to hide completed or cancelled issues, show only assigned or created by you, and to sort by status, priority, nam, or created date. By default, the plugin will hide canceled issues.\\\\n\\\\nFigma Plugin \\\\n\\\\nPrivacy Policy\\\\n\\\\nTerms of Service By authenticating the plugin, you agree to Linear's Terms of Service. Plugin authentication grants you access to Linear's features and functionality.\\\\n\\\\nFigma File Key Transmission When the plugin is opened within Figma, Linear will receive the file key for the current Figma file. When connecting a frame in Figma to a Linear issue, Linear will receive and securely store the file key associated with the respective Figma file. This storage of file keys allows Linear to establish the necessary connection and enable bi-directional linking between Figma and Linear.\\\\n\\\\nFigma File Key Access Any user with access to a Linear issue will also have access to the associated file key for any linked Figma frames.\\\\n\\\\nData Removal Users have the authority to remove Figma data stored by Linear at any time. This can be achieved by either disconnecting the frame from the Linear issue or deleting the associated team or workspace. To permanently delete your data from Linear’s storage contact security@linear.app\\\\n\\\\nData Processing All data, including Figma file keys, is processed by Linear in accordance with Linear’s Data Processing Agreement (DPA). The DPA outlines the measures taken by Linear to ensure the privacy and security of customer data. For more details on our data processing practices, please refer to our DPA.\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\"}]\"}]},\"logs\":[\"[log] {\\n  \\\"content\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"type\\\": \\\"text\\\",\\n      \\\"text\\\": \\\"[{\\\\\\\"id\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"96e92cf0-7141-4395-8660-393a8c6a9ddd\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"title\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"Preferences\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"url\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"https://linear.app/docs/account-preferences\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"snippet\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"Adjust your preferences to help you move faster and personalize your workflow.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nGeneral\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThe general preferences offer high-level adjustments for how you want things to appear in Linear.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nDefault home view\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nThis setting lets you set the default view that opens whenever you close or log out of Linear and re-open/re-login. It defaults to your team's Active issues view, which is the view that shows all issues that are in an Unstarted or Started workflow status.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nTo change your default home view, open the dropdown menu to select from any of these default views:\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nAll issues\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nActive issues\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nCurrent cycle\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nInbox\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nMy Issues\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nFavorited Views\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nFavorited Projects\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nDisplay full names\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nSelect whether you want to see your team members' full names or the username they have personalized in their Profile section.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nFirst day of the week\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nPersonalize how you want your calendars to display your weeks.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nConvert text emoticons into emojis\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nTurn this toggle on to speed up emoji input.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nInterface and theme\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nAdjust your font size and/or opt for a pointer cursor when hovering over interactive elements.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nChoose from our presets or create your own custom theme for your workspace. Select several Light or Dark modes as a preference or choose system preference if you'd like the app to switch based on your computer settings.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nYou can also build your own custom theme. Find and use over 70 themes (and counting!) on the open source site linear.style, where you can click to copy and use them. \\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nDesktop application\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nChoose whether to attempt to open Linear URLs in the desktop app.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nSelect whether you want notification badges on your desktop app icon, or remove them to avoid the noise.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\n...[truncated]\"],\"toolCalls\":[{\"name\":\"EXECUTE_TOOL\",\"args\":{\"tool_id\":\"a7bf0a61-f018-4f75-9375-3e62a100f3df:search_documentation\",\"tool_arguments\":{\"query\":\"workflows\"}},\"durationMs\":494,\"result\":{\"content\":[{\"type\":\"text\",\"text\":\"[{\\\"id\\\":\\\"96e92cf0-7141-4395-8660-393a8c6a9ddd\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Preferences\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/account-preferences\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Adjust your preferences to help you move faster and personalize your workflow.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nGeneral\\\\n\\\\nThe general preferences offer high-level adjustments for how you want things to appear in Linear.\\\\n\\\\nDefault home view\\\\n\\\\nThis setting lets you set the default view that opens whenever you close or log out of Linear and re-open/re-login. It defaults to your team's Active issues view, which is the view that shows all issues that are in an Unstarted or Started workflow status.\\\\n\\\\nTo change your default home view, open the dropdown menu to select from any of these default views:\\\\n\\\\nAll issues\\\\n\\\\nActive issues\\\\n\\\\nCurrent cycle\\\\n\\\\nInbox\\\\n\\\\nMy Issues\\\\n\\\\nFavorited Views\\\\n\\\\nFavorited Projects\\\\n\\\\nDisplay full names\\\\n\\\\nSelect whether you want to see your team members' full names or the username they have personalized in their Profile section.\\\\n\\\\nFirst day of the week\\\\n\\\\nPersonalize how you want your calendars to display your weeks.\\\\n\\\\nConvert text emoticons into emojis\\\\n\\\\nTurn this toggle on to speed up emoji input.\\\\n\\\\nInterface and theme\\\\n\\\\nAdjust your font size and/or opt for a pointer cursor when hovering over interactive elements.\\\\n\\\\nChoose from our presets or create your own custom theme for your workspace. Select several Light or Dark modes as a preference or choose system preference if you'd like the app to switch based on your computer settings.\\\\n\\\\nYou can also build your own custom theme. Find and use over 70 themes (and counting!) on the open source site linear.style, where you can click to copy and use them. \\\\n\\\\nDesktop application\\\\n\\\\nChoose whether to attempt to open Linear URLs in the desktop app.\\\\n\\\\nSelect whether you want notification badges on your desktop app icon, or remove them to avoid the noise.\\\\n\\\\nChoose whether to enable spell check.\\\\n\\\\nAutomations and workflows\\\\n\\\\nAuto-assign to self\\\\n\\\\nOptionally, enable an automation to always assign issues you create to yourself. You can also configure whether you should be auto-assigned to issues you move to a started status.\\\\n\\\\nIf you don't turn these on, you can always toggle the Create more button or use Cmd/Ctrl Shift Enter when creating issues to create more with the same assignee. We don't have a setting to auto-assign issues to specific users. The best way to accomplish that workflow is to create templates with the assignee field set to a specific user.\\\\n\\\\nGit attachment format\\\\n\\\\nDisplay title, or title and repository for your git attachments.\\\\n\\\\nGit-related automations\\\\n\\\\nIf you've enabled Linear's GitHub or GitLab integrations, you'll likely copy the git branch name with Cmd/Ctrl Shift . to link Linear issues to pull requests. You can set two actions to happen whenever you do:\\\\n\\\\nOn git branch copy, move issue to started status which moves the issue to the first workflow status under the started category (often \\\\\\\"In progress\\\\\\\"). \\\\n\\\\nOn git branch copy, auto-assign to yourself\\\\n\\\\nWe highly recommend these settings for any developers using Linear. In combination, they save you a few clicks every time you begin an issue.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"8d26616f-28d7-4210-af91-79da93ea773a\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"GitLab\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/gitlab\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Linear supports linking your Linear issues to GitLab and automates your merge request workflows.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nLink Linear issues to GitLab merge requests. Automate your MR workflow so that issues update when MRs are drafted, opened, merged and when reviews are requested. You can link single or multiple issues to a specific MR.\\\\n\\\\nConfigure the GitLab integration\\\\n\\\\nWe support linking GitLab merge requests for both hosted and self-hosted installations, as long as your installation is publicly available. \\\\n\\\\nNavigate to Linear Settings > Features > Integrations > GitLab\\\\n\\\\nClick Enable to launch the set-up pop-up.\\\\n\\\\nNavigate to GitLab > User Settings > Personal Access Token or GitLab > Project Settings > Access Token to create an API access token.\\\\n\\\\nAn access token is used to query the GitLab API for further information and also used to post issue linkbacks. Because GitLab doesn't support bot accounts, linkbacks are created under the name of the token owner. It's recommended that you create a new user for Linear to act as the bot account.\\\\n\\\\nWhen creating the token, set the api or read_api scope.\\\\n\\\\nIf the read_api scope is selected, Linear will not post linkbacks to the issue on GitLab merge requests. If using a project access token, the token needs Reporter role or higher.\\\\n\\\\n(Optional) Enable and set a custom URL for your self-hosted GitLab installation without any path (e.g. https://gitlab.yourcompany.com). This URL needs to be accessible to public Internet and is only required for self-hosted installations. The earliest supported version of GitLab is 15.6.\\\\n\\\\nIf you need to grant access to Linear's IPs, they are 35.231.147.226, 35.243.134.228, 34.140.253.14, 34.38.87.206, 34.134.222.122, and 35.222.25.142.\\\\n\\\\nClick Connect.\\\\n\\\\nLinear will generate the Webhook URL after it validates the access token. Copy and paste this URL to GitLab in either:\\\\n\\\\nA Group's Webhook Settings (For a company on GitLab's Premium or Ultimate tiers) to integrate all projects under it.\\\\n\\\\nA Project's Webhook Settings, to individually connect a specific project.\\\\n\\\\nEnable the following Triggers:\\\\n\\\\nPush events\\\\n\\\\nComments\\\\n\\\\nMerge request events\\\\n\\\\nPipeline events\\\\n\\\\nUnder SSL verification, ensure Enable SSL verification is checked\\\\n\\\\nClick Save changes.\\\\n\\\\nLink Merge Requests\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new branch\\\\n\\\\nSet the branch format in Linear Settings > Workspace > Integrations GitLab > Branch format. When viewing or selecting a Linear issue, use the Copy git branch name action or shortcut Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + . It will give you a branch name with the issue ID which you can use to create a new branch in GitLab.\\\\n\\\\nAdd the issue ID in the MR title\\\\n\\\\nInclude the Linear issue ID (e.g. ID-123) in the MR title when creating merge requests.\\\\n\\\\nUse a magic word\\\\n\\\\nAdd a magic word + issue ID in the MR description (e.g. Fixes ENG-123). The integration cannot link MRs via comments or commit messages. The available magic words to link issues are: close, closes, closed, closing fix, fixes, fixed, fixing resolve, resolves, resolved, resolving complete, completes, completed, completing.\\\\n\\\\nTo link MRs to issues without them automating your issue status on MR merge, use the one of the contributing magic words: ref, references, part of, related to, contributes to, towards. When using a non-closing magic word like this, the linked MR or commit will still move the issue through other statuses per Workflow settings, but will not automate the issue's status when the MR or commit merges. Instead, it will only transition the issue through to what’s configured prior to the On MR or commit merge event.\\\\n\\\\nLink multiple Linear issues to an MR\\\\n\\\\nTo link multiple Linear issues to a single Merge Request, or to link a Merge Request after creating it, use the magic word linking method by including multiple closing statements in the MR description (e.g. Fixes ENG-123, DES-5 and ENG-256). Linking will happen after you save your changes. The Linear issue will not close until all Merge Requests have been closed/merged.\\\\n\\\\nMerge Request automation\\\\n\\\\nThe merge request automation allows you to select specific statuses for your Linear issues based on MR changes—saving you the hassle of updating Linear issues manually. \\\\n\\\\nCustomize the MR automation in Settings > Team > Workflow. Since this is a team setting, it must be configured for each team in your workspace.\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nReady for merge automation\\\\n\\\\nIf you'd like to use the Merge Request automation to capture passing CI checks, please verify in GitLab that:\\\\n\\\\nYou have enabled Pipeline events under the Webhook settings for any repo part of the GitLab integration.\\\\n\\\\nAll pipelines that Linear should consider when determining if an MR is mergeable need to be merge request pipelines. GitLab documentation is available here.\\\\n\\\\nUnder ‘Merge requests’ in project settings, ‘Pipelines must succeed’ is checked.\\\\n\\\\nCustom merge queues\\\\n\\\\nIf you use a custom merge queue that merges changes and then closes the merge request, be sure to add the externally-merged label before closing it. This tells Linear to treat the merge request as successfully merged even though it was closed.\\\\n\\\\nBranch-specific rules\\\\n\\\\nYou can set custom workflow automations based on particular target branches. For example, you can now configure that when a MR is merged to:\\\\n\\\\nstaging, the issue status should change to “In QA”\\\\n\\\\nmain, the issue status should change to “Deployed”\\\\n\\\\nYou can also override a default rule in a particular branch with “no action” if desired, so that issues linked to a change in that branch will not change status. Branch rules can be specified using regex, e.g: ^fea/.* can set automations for all feature branches.\\\\n\\\\nIssue linkbacks\\\\n\\\\nWhen an issue is linked with a merge request, Linear posts a linkback message as a comment with the issue title and description. All the merge requests are also listed in the issue details in Linear. When your MR is being reviewed in GitLab, see the avatars of up to three most recent reviewers and their review states without leaving Linear. This cross-referencing makes it faster to retain context without jumping between apps. Linkbacks are managed by the scope selected.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-assign and update status\\\\n\\\\nSave yourself a few steps by toggling on our automations in Settings > My Account > Preferences > Behavior.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-assigns the issue to you, and/or\\\\n\\\\nMove the issue to the first Started status (which you can customize in Settings > Team > Workflow) when you copy the git branch name.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-linking\\\\n\\\\nGitLab supports rendering URLs pointing to Linear when you mention issue IDs in GitLab. To configure this functionality, please reference GitLab's documentation. \\\\n\\\\nFAQ\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"87abf250-960d-4a50-85cf-c67aa69ded11\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Issue status\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/configuring-workflows\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Set statuses that your issues will move through on each team.\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nIssues statuses define the type and order of states that issues can move through from start to completion. These workflows are team-specific and come with a default set and order: Backlog > Todo > In Progress > Done > Canceled. \\\\n\\\\nConfigure\\\\n\\\\nAdd or edit statuses in a team from Settings -> Teams -> Issue statuses & automations. You will see a list of all statuses in that team and their order. Click the three dots next to each status and select Edit to make changes to the name, color, or description. Create statuses by clicking the + button, or remove statuses (as long as at least one status exists in each category). \\\\n\\\\nStatuses can be rearranged within a category but categories cannot be moved around. To change the order of a status, drag it to a new position.\\\\n\\\\nDefault status\\\\n\\\\nThe default status defines the workflow status that will be applied to newly created issues in your team. This can be overridden by the user when creating an issue, but makes it a lot easier to track and organize new issues that come in. By default, your first Backlog status will be the default status. To change that, hover over a different status in the Backlog or Todo categories and then select Make default.\\\\n\\\\nDuplicate issue status\\\\n\\\\nWhen you mark an issue as a duplicate of another, its status will change to Canceled. You can create a custom status in the Canceled category (e.g. Duplicate) and then configure workflow settings so that the custom status is applied instead. \\\\n\\\\nTriage\\\\n\\\\nTriage is an additional status category that acts as an Inbox for your team. Triage is particularly powerful when combined with other integrations like Asks, Slack, or our support ticketing integrations. Learn more here.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-close and auto-archive\\\\n\\\\nWhen enabled, auto-close will close issues that have not been updated in a set period of time.\\\\n\\\\nAuto-archive controls when issues in the team will be archived. When an issue archives, its creator will be notified—this is an opportunity to unarchive if the issue is still relevant. Archived issues are still searchable and restorable in the future.\\\\n\\\\nArchiving is only automatic and is not available as a manual action. More information about archiving is available here.\\\\n\\\\n\\\"},{\\\"id\\\":\\\"ce5ff110-a02b-450e-be48-1fcddba7dbc9\\\",\\\"title\\\":\\\"Integration Directory\\\",\\\"url\\\":\\\"https://linear.app/docs/integration-directory\\\",\\\"snippet\\\":\\\"Discover Linear add-ons or build your own\\\\n\\\\nOverview\\\\n\\\\nLinear's Integration Directory features apps and add-ons created by the Linear team as well as external applications. claim_scores.claim: The response includes documentation about workflows

claims: The response lists cycles
The response indicates whether any cycles are active
tasks.prompt: List all cycles in Linear and tell me if there are any active ones.

task_result.final_answer:
Based on the information from your Linear workspace, I found one team named "Kontext". However, after checking that team, it appears there are no cycles configured for it.

tasks.prompt: Search Linear docs for how to create an issue.

task_result.final_answer:
You can create an issue in Linear in several ways:

### From the Linear App

*   **Keyboard Shortcut:** The quickest way is to press the `C` key to open the issue creation modal. For a full-screen editor, use `V`.
*   **From a Template:** To use a pre-defined template, press `Option/Alt + C`.
*   **New Issue Button:** Click the "Create new issue" icon in the top-left corner of the app.
*   **From Highlighted Text:** If you highlight any text on your screen and then press `C`, the highlighted text will automatically become the issue title.

### From Your Browser

*   Simply type `linear.new` into your browser's URL bar. This will take you directly to the new issue page in your logged-in Linear workspace.
*   You can also pre-fill issue details by adding query parameters to the URL. For example:
    `https://linear.new?title=Fix+the+login+bug&status=Todo&priority=Urgent`

### Via Email

*   You can create issues by sending an email to a unique address associated with a specific team. This can be set up in your team's settings under **Settings > Teams > [Your Team] > General > Create by email**.

### Other Methods

*   **Recurring Issues:** For tasks that repeat, you can set up recurring issues that are created automatically on a schedule you define.
*   **Integrations:** You can create issues from other applications like Slack, Zapier, or through the GraphQL API for custom workflows.

It will redirect you to the new issue creation page as long as you are logged into your Linear account.\\\\n\\\\nIssues can be created using our GraphQL API. Many integrations in our directory allow creating issues from other services using this functionality.\\\\n\\\\nIf you have text highlighted when you go to create an issue, this will pre-fill in the issue title.\\\\n\\\\nCreate an issue via email\\\\n\\\\nIssues can be created by sending an email to a unique intake email address that routes to a team. Replies sent on the original email to the forwarding address will not create additional issues. \\\\n\\\\nCreate recurring issues\\\\n\\\\nYou can create recurring issues to automate your repeated tasks on a cadence of your choosing.\\\\n\\\\nCreate a recurring issue\\\\n\\\\nWhen creating a new issue, you can choose to make it into recurring in the issue composer using the …  menu and choosing \\\\\\\"Make recurring…\\\\\\\"\\\\n\\\\nConvert an existing issue into a recurring issue\\\\n\\\\nTo convert any issue into a recurring issue, open the issue and in the …  menu choose Convert into > Recurring issue…\\\\nYou can also use the Cmd/Ctrl + K menu by typing \\\\\\\"Convert into recurring issue\\\\\\\".\\\\nYou can then choose your first due date, and the cadence at which it repeats.\\\\n\\\\nCreate recurring issues in team settings\\\\n\\\\nNavigate to Team settings > Recurring issues.\\\\nTo create a new recurring issue, click the + icon and set your chosen schedule of recurrence.\\\\n\\\\nCreate recurring issues from templates\\\\n\\\\nIf you have an existing issue template you want to turn into a recurring issue you can do so. \\\\n\\\\nFirst create your issue and apply your chosen template. \\\\nOnce created, choose the options to convert to a recurring issue from the … menu or command menu. This issue will now turn into a recurring issue, and include any properties that were in the template, including sub-issues.\\\\n\\\\nRecurring issues can easily be found from your Team settings > Recurring issues, or filtering your views using the Recurring issues filter.\\\\n\\\\nCadence of recurring issues\\\\n\\\\nOnce you create a recurring issue, future issues in the cadence are expected to be created once the due date passes (00:01 the following day in your team’s timezone.)\\\\n\\\\nCreate a new issue URL\\\\n\\\\nThe following links trigger the creation of a new Linear issue in any setting.\\\\n\\\\nUse & between each field or issue property when creating a string of pre-settings.\\\\n\\\\nApply pre-set properties\\\\n\\\\nWe support the following query UUID, display name/name of the user, or assignee=me to assign the creator\\\\n\\\\nFor example, https://linear.new?assignee=john or https://linear.new?assignee=Erin+Baker or https://linear.new?assignee=me.\\\\n\\\\nestimate\\\\n\\\\nCan be set by their point number e.g. estimate=4\\\\n\\\\nT-shirt sizes have the following point values: No priority (0), XS (1), S(2), M (3), L (5), XL (8), XXL (13), XXXL (21)\\\\n\\\\nFor example, https://linear.app/team/LIN/new?estimate=2\\\\n\\\\ncycle\\\\n\\\\nCan be set by UUID, cycle number of a name of a cycle\\\\n\\\\nFor example, https://linear.app/team/MOB?cycle=36 or https://linear.new/team/EU/new?cycle=focus+on+bugs\\\\n\\\\nlabel\\\\n\\\\nUse a comma between each label you want to apply\\\\n\\\\nFor example,
